topics.title: Introduction to Computer Networks: Network Components, Network Models, ISO/OSI, TCP/IP, Physical Topology, Overview of the Internet, Protocol Layering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST724 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Computer Networks: Network Components, Network Models, ISO/OSI, TCP/IP, Physical Topology, Overview of the Internet, Protocol Layering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Computer Network?

A **Computer Network** is an interconnected collection of autonomous computing devices (hosts, end-systems, routers, switches) that communicate with one another over a shared transmission medium using well-defined **protocols** (rules and conventions) to exchange data and share resources.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Standard):** "A computer network is a set of devices connected by communication links, where the devices communicate through protocols to share hardware, software, and data resources."

### Conceptual Analogy — The Highway System
Imagine a country with millions of citizens who need to send letters to each other. The **postal system** provides:
- **Roads (Links)** — physical paths,
- **Vehicles (Signals)** — what carries the letter,
- **Postal Rules (Protocols)** — addressing, sorting, delivery standards,
- **Post Offices (Routers/Switches)** — intermediate routing points.

A computer network is essentially the **digital postal system** of the modern world. Just as a letter must follow a standard format, data must follow a **protocol** so that any device, anywhere, can understand it.

## 1.2 Network Components

The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies every network into **five fundamental building blocks**:

| # | Component | Role | Real-World Analogy |
|---|-----------|------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Hosts / End Devices** | Sources or sinks of data (PC, mobile, server) | Houses sending/receiving letters |
| 2 | **Transmission Media** | Wired (copper, fibre) or wireless (RF) | Roads and rail lines |
| 3 | **Intermediate Devices** | Routers, switches, hubs, gateways, firewalls | Post offices, toll booths |
| 4 | **Protocols (Software)** | Rules (TCP, IP, HTTP, FTP) | Postal regulations, language |
| 5 | **Network Services / Applications** | E-mail, web, VoIP, video | Letter contents |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically emphasizes distinguishing between **Hardware Components** (devices + media) and **Software Components** (protocols + services). Examiners often test this 2-way classification.

## 1.3 Why Networks? — Motivation & Goals

A network is built to satisfy three core objectives:

1. **Resource Sharing** — sharing expensive hardware (printers, storage) and software.
2. **Communication** — e-mail, instant messaging, video calls.
3. **Distributed Computing** — multiple machines cooperating on a single task (cloud, grids).

## 1.4 Network Criteria (Forouzas's 5 Performance Metrics)

For a network to be considered **efficient and effective**, it must meet these criteria (frequently asked in KTU 2-mark questions):

- **Performance** — measured by *Throughput* and *Delay* (Latency).
- **Reliability** — measured by *Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)*.
- **Security** — confidentiality, integrity, and availability of data.
- **Scalability** — ability to grow without performance collapse.
- **Fairness** — equal opportunity for all users to access resources.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Term — Bandwidth:** Theoretical maximum data rate a link can carry, measured in **bits per second (bps)**. Modern networks operate in the order of **Gbps** to **Tbps**.
>
> **Key Term — Throughput:** Actual data rate achieved, always **less than or equal to** bandwidth.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Network Models — The Need for Layered Architecture

A **network model** (or network architecture) is a structured blueprint that defines how data is transmitted, processed, and received across a network. Without layering, communication would be a chaotic, monolithic, hardware-dependent mess.

### 2.1.1 Why Layering?

Layering provides four indispensable benefits:

- **Modularity** — each layer has one well-defined job.
- **Abstraction** — upper layers do not worry about lower-layer mechanics.
- **Interoperability** — vendors can mix hardware/software.
- **Easier troubleshooting** — failures can be isolated to a specific layer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **layer** in a network model provides services to the layer above it, and **requests services** from the layer below. Each layer talks logically to its *peer* on the other machine but physically communicates *vertically* down the stack — this is the **peer-to-peer virtual communication** concept.

## 2.2 ISO/OSI Reference Model — The 7-Layer Standard

The **Open Systems Interconnection (OSI)** model was developed by **ISO (International Organization for Standardization)** in **1984** as a theoretical reference. It has **7 layers**, often memorized with the mnemonic:

> *"All People Seem To Need Data Processing"* (from top → bottom: **A P S T N D P**)

### Layer-by-Layer Breakdown

| # | Layer | Data Unit (PDU) | Core Function | Example Protocols / Devices |
|---|-------|-----------------|---------------|------------------------------|
| 7 | **Application** | Message / Data | User interface, network services | HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, SNMP |
| 6 | **Presentation** | Message | Translation, encryption, compression | SSL/TLS, JPEG, MPEG, ASCII |
| 5 | **Session** | Message | Establish/manage/terminate sessions | NetBIOS, RPC, PPTP |
| 4 | **Transport** | Segment (TCP) / Datagram (UDP) | Reliable end-to-end delivery, port addressing | TCP, UDP |
| 3 | **Network** | Packet | Logical addressing and routing | IP, ICMP, OSPF, BGP, Router |
| 2 | **Data Link** | Frame | Node-to-node framing, MAC addressing, error detection | Ethernet, Wi-Fi (802.11), Switch |
| 1 | **Physical** | Bit | Raw bit transmission over medium | Cables, connectors, Hubs, Modem |

### Detailed Function Description (KTU High-Yield)

- **Layer 7 — Application:** Closest to the user. Enables services like web browsing, file transfer, e-mail.
- **Layer 6 — Presentation:** Data format translation (EBCDIC ↔ ASCII), encryption/decryption, compression.
- **Layer 5 — Session:** Establishes, maintains, synchronizes dialogues between two hosts. Handles checkpoints and recovery.
- **Layer 4 — Transport:** The **heart of the OSI model**. Provides either connection-oriented (TCP) or connectionless (UDP) service. **Port numbers** live here.
- **Layer 3 — Network:** **Routing**. Uses **IP addresses** to find the best path. Routers operate here.
- **Layer 2 — Data Link:** Converts bits into **frames**. Uses **MAC addresses** (48-bit hardware address). Provides error **detection** (not correction, in most cases). Bridges & switches operate here.
- **Layer 1 — Physical:** Defines voltages, pin layouts, cable specs, transmission rates. Hubs and repeaters operate here.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** "**Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away**" — bottom-up mnemonic: **P D N T S P A**.

## 2.3 TCP/IP Model (Internet Protocol Suite) — The 4-Layer Practical Model

The **TCP/IP model** (also called the **Internet Protocol Suite**) was developed by the **U.S. Department of Defense (DARPA)** in the **1970s**, and is the **foundation of today's Internet**. It is more pragmatic than OSI and has **4 or 5 layers** depending on the source. KTU uses the **5-layer hybrid model**.

### 2.3.1 The 5-Layer Hybrid Model (KTU Standard)

| # | Layer | PDU | Mapping to OSI | Key Protocols |
|---|-------|-----|----------------|----------------|
| 5 | **Application** | Message | OSI 7 + 6 + 5 | HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, DHCP |
| 4 | **Transport** | Segment / Datagram | OSI 4 | TCP, UDP |
| 3 | **Network (Internet)** | Datagram / Packet | OSI 3 | IP, ICMP, ARP, RARP |
| 2 | **Data Link (Network Access)** | Frame | OSI 2 | Ethernet, Wi-Fi, PPP |
| 1 | **Physical** | Bit | OSI 1 | Cables, connectors, radio waves |

### 2.3.2 TCP/IP — Key Protocol Functions

- **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol):** **Connection-oriented**, reliable, byte-stream, full-duplex. Uses **3-way handshake** (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK). Sends acknowledgments and re-transmits lost segments.
- **UDP (User Datagram Protocol):** **Connectionless**, unreliable, low-overhead, best-effort. Used for streaming, DNS, VoIP.
- **IP (Internet Protocol):** **Connectionless**, best-effort **packet forwarding**. Two versions: **IPv4 (32-bit)** and **IPv6 (128-bit)**.

## 2.4 OSI vs TCP/IP — Critical Comparison (KTU Favourite)

| Feature | OSI Model | TCP/IP Model |
|---------|-----------|---------------|
| Layers | 7 | 4 or 5 |
| Origin | ISO | DARPA |
| Adoption | Theoretical reference | Practical implementation |
| Session & Presentation | Separate layers | Merged into Application |
| Network Access | Split (Physical + Data Link) | Combined |
| Protocol Dependency | Protocol-independent | Protocol-centric |
| Rigidity | Strict layer boundaries | Loose, flexible |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Point:** TCP/IP became dominant because it was **practical, simple, and proven** by ARPANET — the precursor to the Internet. OSI is taught as a **conceptual reference** to understand layered design.

## 2.5 Physical Topology

**Physical topology** refers to the actual geometric arrangement of nodes and links in a network. It is the **layout you can physically see**.

### 2.5.1 The 7 Standard Physical Topologies

| Topology | Diagram Shape | Characteristics | Pros | Cons |
|----------|---------------|-----------------|------|------|
| **Bus** | Single backbone cable | All nodes share a common line; uses terminators | Cheap, simple | Single point of failure, collisions |
| **Star** | Central hub/switch | All nodes connect to a central device | Easy to install, one cable failure doesn't kill the network | Hub failure kills all |
| **Ring** | Closed loop | Data travels in one direction (or both in dual ring) | Predictable performance | One node break disrupts ring |
| **Mesh** | Every node connected to every other | Full (n(n-1)/2 links) or partial | Maximum redundancy, robust | Expensive, complex |
| **Tree (Hierarchical)** | Root → branches → leaves | Combination of bus and star | Scalable, structured | Root failure = catastrophic |
| **Hybrid** | Mix of two or more | Flexible, custom | Adaptable | Complex design |
| **Point-to-Point** | Direct link between two | Simplest | Private, fast | Cannot scale |

### 2.5.2 Physical vs Logical Topology

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical topology** = cable layout.
> **Logical topology** = how data flows regardless of cabling.
>
> **Example:** Ethernet over a *star* physical layout is logically a *bus*. Token Ring over a *star* physical layout is logically a *ring*.

## 2.6 Overview of the Internet

The **Internet** is a global, public, packet-switched network of networks that uses the **TCP/IP protocol suite**.

### 2.6.1 Key Internet Components

- **ISPs (Internet Service Providers):** Tier 1, Tier 2, Tier 3.
- **IXPs (Internet Exchange Points):** Where ISPs interconnect.
- **NAPs (Network Access Points):** Major Internet hubs.
- **Backbone:** High-speed, long-distance fiber links.
- **DNS (Domain Name System):** Translates names to IP addresses.
- **WWW, Email, FTP, etc.:** Services running on top.

### 2.6.2 The Internetwork Concept

```
End System → LAN → ISP → Backbone → ISP → LAN → End System
```

The Internet is essentially a **network of networks** — a meta-network.

## 2.7 Protocol Layering — The Foundation

**Protocol layering** is the principle of organizing network communication into **independent functional layers**, each with its own protocols, services, and interfaces.

### 2.7.1 Three Principles of Layering (Forouza)

1. Each layer performs a **specific** function.
2. The function of each layer is chosen with **international standards protocols** in mind.
3. The layer boundaries are chosen to **minimize information flow across interfaces**.

### 2.7.2 Encapsulation & Decapsulation (HIGH-YIELD)

When data moves **down** the sending stack, each layer **adds a header** (and sometimes a trailer) — this is **encapsulation**. When it moves **up** the receiving stack, headers are **stripped** — this is **decapsulation**.

- Application → Transport → Network → Data Link → Physical
- Data → **Segment** → **Datagram/Packet** → **Frame** → **Bit stream**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse **PDUs**. The PDU **renames** as it descends the stack:
> - Application/Transport: **Message / Segment**
> - Network: **Packet / Datagram**
> - Data Link: **Frame**
> - Physical: **Bits**

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

This is the *quantitative* cheat sheet for Module 1. Most topics here are descriptive, but the following formulas are tested directly.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variable Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|----------------------|------------------|-------|
| 1 | **Propagation Delay** | $T_{prop} = \dfrac{D}{V}$ | $D$ = link length, $V$ = propagation speed ($\approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s in fibre) | seconds (s) |
| 2 | **Transmission Delay** | $T_{trans} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | $L$ = packet length (bits), $R$ = link bandwidth (bps) | seconds (s) |
| 3 | **End-to-End Delay** | $T_{end} = T_{proc} + T_{queue} + T_{trans} + T_{prop} + T_{proc}$ | All above delays combined | seconds (s) |
| 4 | **Throughput (Simple)** | $\text{Throughput} = \min(R_1, R_2, \dots, R_n)$ | Bottleneck link rate | bps |
| 5 | **Full Mesh Links** | $L = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | $n$ = number of nodes | number of links |
| 6 | **Bandwidth-Delay Product** | $BDP = R \times T_{prop}$ | Max data "in flight" on a link | bits |
| 7 | **IPv4 Address Space** | $N = 2^{32}$ | Total IPv4 addresses | $4.29 \times 10^9$ |
| 8 | **IPv6 Address Space** | $N = 2^{128}$ | Total IPv6 addresses | $\approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ |
| 9 | **Maximum Frame Rate** | $f_{max} = \dfrac{R}{\text{Frame Size}}$ | Packets per second (pps) | packets/s |
| 10 | **Round Trip Time (RTT)** | $RTT = 2 \times T_{prop} + 2 \times T_{trans} + T_{proc}$ | Time for packet to reach destination and ACK to return | seconds (s) |

> [!WARNING]
> When solving problems, remember **Propagation speed in copper/fiber ≈ $2 \times 10^8$ m/s** (not $3 \times 10^8$ m/s, which is the speed of light in vacuum). This is a common KTU exam trap.

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecommunications:** 5G, LTE, and DSL all use layered models derived from OSI/TCP-IP.
- **Cloud Computing:** Data centers use the 5-layer hybrid model to virtualize network services.
- **Embedded Systems (IoT):** MQTT and CoAP are layer-7 protocols used in sensor networks.
- **Cybersecurity:** Encryption (TLS) operates at the **Presentation layer** (OSI) or the **Application layer** (TCP/IP).
- **Network Troubleshooting:** The **OSI model is the diagnostic ladder** — "ping" tests Layer 3, "traceroute" tests Layer 3, packet sniffers test Layer 2.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Layered Functions Compared (Numerical-Reasoning View)

This matrix expresses the **design function** of each layer in terms of **what changes** versus **what stays the same** during transmission. It is a common KTU analytical question.

| Layer | What changes (per hop) | What stays constant (end-to-end) | Typical Address Used | Error Handling |
|-------|------------------------|-----------------------------------|----------------------|----------------|
| Application | Format / Encryption | Meaning of data | URL, Email ID | Application-specific |
| Transport | Port addition | Port numbers, flow control | Port # (16-bit) | ACK, retransmission (TCP) |
| Network | IP header updated (TTL, src/dst) | Destination IP address | IP address (32/128-bit) | None (best-effort) |
| Data Link | MAC addresses swapped | Frame structure, payload | MAC address (48-bit) | CRC, checksum (detection) |
| Physical | Voltage / light pulses | Bit timing, encoding | None (just bits) | None |

> [!NOTE]
> The **destination IP address remains unchanged** end-to-end, but the **source and destination MAC addresses** change **at every hop**. This is a classic KTU 1-mark conceptual trap.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation — End-to-End Delay (Worked Example)

The **End-to-End delay** of a packet is the sum of all the delays it encounters from source to destination. We will derive the formula step by step.

### Step 1: Identify Delay Components

A packet experiences **four fundamental delays** plus processing:

1. **Processing Delay** ($T_{proc}$) — Time to examine the packet header and decide where to forward. Typically microseconds.
2. **Queuing Delay** ($T_{queue}$) — Time spent waiting in the router's buffer. Variable, depends on congestion.
3. **Transmission Delay** ($T_{trans}$) — Time to push all bits of the packet onto the link.
4. **Propagation Delay** ($T_{prop}$) — Time for one bit to travel from sender to receiver.

### Step 2: Derive Transmission Delay

Consider a packet of length $L$ bits and a link of bandwidth $R$ bits per second.

The number of bits pushed per second is $R$. Therefore, the time to push $L$ bits is:

$$T_{trans} = \frac{L}{R}$$

**Reasoning:** If $L = 1000$ bits and $R = 10^6$ bps, then $T_{trans} = 10^{-3}$ s = 1 ms.

### Step 3: Derive Propagation Delay

A signal travels at velocity $V$ m/s through a medium of length $D$ m. The time for the first bit to reach the other end is:

$$T_{prop} = \frac{D}{V}$$

**Reasoning:** For fibre, $V \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s. For a 1000 km link, $T_{prop} = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ s = 5 ms.

### Step 4: Combine All Delays

The **total end-to-end delay** for one packet across **N** router hops is:

$$T_{end} = N \cdot (T_{proc} + T_{queue} + T_{trans} + T_{prop}) + T_{proc\_sender}$$

Or simplified for a single-router, single-link scenario:

$$T_{end} = T_{proc} + T_{queue} + T_{trans} + T_{prop} + T_{proc}$$

### Step 5: Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A host sends a file of **1,000,000 bits** over a **2 Mbps** link. The link length is **5,000 km**. Propagation speed is $2 \times 10^8$ m/s. Calculate:
1. Transmission delay
2. Propagation delay
3. End-to-end delay (ignoring processing and queuing)

**Given:** $L = 10^6$ bits, $R = 2 \times 10^6$ bps, $D = 5 \times 10^6$ m, $V = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s.

**Step (a) — Transmission Delay:**

$$T_{trans} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{10^{6}}{2 \times 10^{6}} = 0.5 \text{ s}$$

**Step (b) — Propagation Delay:**

$$T_{prop} = \frac{D}{V} = \frac{5 \times 10^{6}}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 0.025 \text{ s}$$

**Step (c) — End-to-End Delay:**

$$T_{end} = T_{trans} + T_{prop} = 0.5 + 0.025 = 0.525 \text{ s}$$

**Interpretation:** Transmission delay dominates for short links with low bandwidth, while propagation delay dominates for long-distance, high-bandwidth links. For a trans-Pacific fibre (e.g., 10,000 km, 100 Gbps, 1 KB packet), propagation delay is ~50 ms vs transmission delay of ~0.00008 ms — **5 orders of magnitude difference**.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation — Full Mesh Link Count (Topology Formula)

### Step 1: Problem Setup

In a **full mesh topology** with $n$ nodes, every node has a direct link to every other node.

### Step 2: Count the Links

The first node connects to $n-1$ other nodes. The second node (excluding the link to the first) connects to $n-2$ new nodes. This continues until the second-to-last node, which connects to 1 new node.

$$L = (n-1) + (n-2) + (n-3) + \dots + 1$$

### Step 3: Apply Arithmetic Series

The sum of the first $k$ natural numbers is $\frac{k(k+1)}{2}$. Here $k = n-1$:

$$L = \frac{(n-1)(n)}{2} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$$

**Step 4: Worked Example**

For $n = 5$ nodes:

$$L = \frac{5 \times 4}{2} = 10 \text{ links}$$

This exponential growth is why full mesh is **impractical for large networks** — the Internet uses **partial mesh** instead.

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Derivation — Bandwidth-Delay Product

### Step 1: Concept

The **Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP)** represents the **maximum number of bits that can be "in flight"** on a link at any instant — i.e., the volume of the link treated as a pipe.

### Step 2: Derivation

If a bit takes $T_{prop}$ seconds to traverse the link, and the link rate is $R$ bps, the sender can push $R$ bits per second, so by the time the first bit arrives, $R \times T_{prop}$ bits have been pushed.

$$\boxed{BDP = R \times T_{prop}}$$

### Step 3: Worked Example

$R = 1$ Gbps, link length $D = 4000$ km, $V = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s.

$$T_{prop} = \frac{4 \times 10^{6}}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 0.02 \text{ s}$$

$$BDP = 10^{9} \times 0.02 = 2 \times 10^{7} \text{ bits} = 20 \text{ Mbits} = 2.5 \text{ MB}$$

**Engineering Utility:** BDP determines the optimal **TCP window size**. If the window is smaller than BDP, the link is underutilized. Modern operating systems auto-tune TCP windows using this principle.

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Derivation — Round Trip Time (RTT)

### Step 1: Definition

RTT is the time from sending a packet to receiving its acknowledgment. It applies to protocols like TCP that use acknowledgments.

### Step 2: Derivation

For a single packet on a single link (no queuing, no processing):

$$RTT = T_{trans\,(outbound)} + T_{prop\,(outbound)} + T_{prop\,(return)} + T_{trans\,(ACK)} + T_{proc}$$

Assuming symmetric link and ignoring the tiny ACK transmission time and processing:

$$RTT \approx 2 \times (T_{trans} + T_{prop})$$

### Step 3: Worked Example

If a packet of 1000 bits is sent over a 1 Mbps link spanning 1000 km:

$$T_{trans} = \frac{1000}{10^{6}} = 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ ms}$$

$$T_{prop} = \frac{10^{6}}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 5 \text{ ms}$$

$$RTT \approx 2 \times (1 + 5) = 12 \text{ ms}$$

## 3.6 Step-by-Step Logical Derivation — Encapsulation Walkthrough

This is a **must-know for KTU** because the question "Trace a packet through the layers" appears almost every year.

### Step 1: Sender Side (Top-Down)

1. **Application layer** creates the user data (e.g., HTTP GET request) — call this **Data**.
2. **Transport layer** (e.g., TCP) adds a header containing source/dest port, sequence number — now it's a **Segment**.
3. **Network layer** (IP) adds its header (src/dest IP, TTL) — now it's a **Datagram / Packet**.
4. **Data Link layer** (Ethernet) adds its header (src/dest MAC) and a **trailer (CRC)** — now it's a **Frame**.
5. **Physical layer** converts the frame into a stream of **bits** and transmits them as voltage/pulses.

### Step 2: Receiver Side (Bottom-Up)

1. **Physical layer** receives bits, reconstructs the **Frame**, hands to Layer 2.
2. **Data Link layer** checks CRC, removes header/trailer, hands **Datagram** to Layer 3.
3. **Network layer** checks IP, decrements TTL, updates routing, hands **Segment** to Layer 4.
4. **Transport layer** reassembles segments, checks ports, hands **Data** to Layer 7.
5. **Application layer** delivers to the user.

### Step 3: Visual Encapsulation (Text Form)

```
[ APP HDR | Data ]                              ← Message
[ TCP HDR | APP HDR | Data ]                    ← Segment
[ IP HDR | TCP HDR | APP HDR | Data ]           ← Datagram
[ ETH HDR | IP HDR | TCP HDR | APP HDR | Data | CRC ]   ← Frame
1010011101010...                                 ← Bits on wire
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Conceptual Architecture of a Computer Network

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef host fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F6FEB,stroke-width:2px,color:#0B2545
    classDef interm fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#F18F01,stroke-width:2px,color:#7A4F01
    classDef media fill:#E6F8E0,stroke:#2E933C,stroke-width:2px,color:#0F3D1E
    classDef serv fill:#F4E6F8,stroke:#7E3FBB,stroke-width:2px,color:#3A1A56

    node1["Host A<br/>Source End System"]:::host
    node2["Host B<br/>Destination End System"]:::host
    node3["Router R1<br/>Layer 3 Device"]:::interm
    node4["Switch S1<br/>Layer 2 Device"]:::interm
    node5["Hub H1<br/>Layer 1 Device"]:::interm
    node6["Server Farm<br/>Application Services"]:::serv
    node7["Transmission Media<br/>Copper / Fibre / Wireless"]:::media
    node8["Protocol Stack<br/>TCP IP HTTP DNS"]:::serv

    node1 -->|Frame| node5
    node5 -->|Bit Stream| node7
    node7 -->|Bit Stream| node4
    node4 -->|Frame| node3
    node3 -->|Packet| node7
    node7 -->|Bit Stream| node2
    node6 -.->|Services| node1
    node8 -.->|Rules| node1
    node8 -.->|Rules| node3

    note1["Layered Protocols Govern<br/>All Communication"]:::serv
    node8 --- note1
```

> **Reading Guide:** Hosts use protocols to format data; intermediate devices (Hub → Switch → Router) move it forward; media physically carry the bits; servers deliver applications. Every block maps to a syllabus topic.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — The 7-Layer OSI Model with Data Units (PDU Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef layer7 fill:#FFE5E5,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    classDef layer6 fill:#FFE5CC,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef layer5 fill:#FFF4CC,stroke:#F9A825,color:#000
    classDef layer4 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000
    classDef layer3 fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000
    classDef layer2 fill:#EDE7F6,stroke:#4527A0,color:#000
    classDef layer1 fill:#ECEFF1,stroke:#37474F,color:#000

    nodeL7["Layer 7<br/>Application<br/>PDU: Message"]:::layer7
    nodeL6["Layer 6<br/>Presentation<br/>PDU: Message"]:::layer6
    nodeL5["Layer 5<br/>Session<br/>PDU: Message"]:::layer5
    nodeL4["Layer 4<br/>Transport<br/>PDU: Segment or Datagram"]:::layer4
    nodeL3["Layer 3<br/>Network<br/>PDU: Packet"]:::layer3
    nodeL2["Layer 2<br/>Data Link<br/>PDU: Frame"]:::layer2
    nodeL1["Layer 1<br/>Physical<br/>PDU: Bit"]:::layer1

    nodeL7 --> nodeL6
    nodeL6 --> nodeL5
    nodeL5 --> nodeL4
    nodeL4 --> nodeL3
    nodeL3 --> nodeL2
    nodeL2 --> nodeL1
```

> **Mnemonic Embedded:** The colour gradient shifts from **red (top)** to **grey (bottom)** to mirror the canonical OSI diagram students draw in exams.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — The 5-Layer TCP/IP Hybrid Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef app fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    classDef trn fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#0277BD,color:#000
    classDef net fill:#81D4FA,stroke:#0288D1,color:#000
    classDef dl fill:#4FC3F7,stroke:#039BE5,color:#000
    classDef ph fill:#29B6F6,stroke:#03A9F4,color:#000

    nodeA["Application<br/>HTTP FTP SMTP DNS<br/>PDU: Message"]:::app
    nodeB["Transport<br/>TCP UDP<br/>PDU: Segment or Datagram"]:::trn
    nodeC["Network Internet<br/>IP ICMP ARP<br/>PDU: Datagram"]:::net
    nodeD["Data Link<br/>Ethernet WiFi PPP<br/>PDU: Frame"]:::dl
    nodeE["Physical<br/>Cables Wireless<br/>PDU: Bit"]:::ph

    nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeC --> nodeD --> nodeE
```

> **Mapping Note:** OSI Layers 5, 6, 7 collapse into the TCP/IP **Application** layer. OSI Layers 1 and 2 are split into the TCP/IP **Network Access** pair (Physical + Data Link).

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Physical Topologies

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef star fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000
    classDef bus fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000

    subgraph STAR["Star Topology"]
        nodeCenter["Hub or Switch"]:::star
        nodeS1["Node 1"]:::star
        nodeS2["Node 2"]:::star
        nodeS3["Node 3"]:::star
        nodeS4["Node 4"]:::star
        nodeCenter --- nodeS1
        nodeCenter --- nodeS2
        nodeCenter --- nodeS3
        nodeCenter --- nodeS4
    end

    subgraph BUS["Bus Topology"]
        nodeB1["Node 1"]:::bus
        nodeB2["Node 2"]:::bus
        nodeB3["Node 3"]:::bus
        nodeB4["Node 4"]:::bus
        nodeB5["Backbone"]:::bus
        nodeB1 --- nodeB5
        nodeB2 --- nodeB5
        nodeB3 --- nodeB5
        nodeB4 --- nodeB5
    end

    subgraph RING["Ring Topology"]
        nodeR1["Node 1"]:::ring
        nodeR2["Node 2"]:::ring
        nodeR3["Node 3"]:::ring
        nodeR4["Node 4"]:::ring
        nodeR1 --- nodeR2
        nodeR2 --- nodeR3
        nodeR3 --- nodeR4
        nodeR4 --- nodeR1
    end

    subgraph MESH["Full Mesh Topology n equals 4"]
        nodeM1["Node 1"]:::mesh
        nodeM2["Node 2"]:::mesh
        nodeM3["Node 3"]:::mesh
        nodeM4["Node 4"]:::mesh
        nodeM1 --- nodeM2
        nodeM1 --- nodeM3
        nodeM1 --- nodeM4

        nodeM2 --- nodeM4
        nodeM3 --- nodeM4

    subgraph TREE["Tree Topology"]
        nodeT0["Root"]:::tree

        nodeTB["Branch B"]:::tree

        nodeT2["Leaf 2"]:::tree
        nodeT3["Leaf 3"]:::tree
        nodeT4["Leaf 4"]:::tree
        nodeT0 --- nodeTA
        nodeT0 --- nodeTB
        nodeTA --- nodeT1
        nodeTA --- nodeT2
        nodeTB --- nodeT3
        nodeTB --- nodeT4
    end
```

> **Exam Tip:** This single diagram covers all the topologies examiners can ask about. A reproduction of even two of these structures in a 14-mark answer usually fetches full marks.

## 4.5 Block Diagram — End-to-End Communication Flow Across the Internet

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef host fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F6FEB,color:#000
    classDef isp fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#F18F01,color:#000
    classDef back fill:#F4E6F8,stroke:#7E3FBB,color:#000

    nodeH1["Host A<br/>PC or Laptop"]:::host
    nodeLAN1["LAN A<br/>Switch and Router"]:::host
    nodeISP1["ISP 1<br/>Local Access"]:::isp
    nodeBB["Internet Backbone<br/>High Speed Core"]:::back
    nodeISP2["ISP 2<br/>Local Access"]:::isp

    nodeH2["Host B<br/>Server"]:::host

    nodeLAN1 --> nodeISP1
    nodeISP1 --> nodeBB
```

> **Reading Guide:** This is the canonical "network of networks" picture. The Internet is not a single network but an **interconnection of independently operated networks**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Computer Network. List the five network components as per the KTU syllabus. *(2 marks for definition, 1 mark for the list.)* [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

A computer network is a collection of autonomous computing devices interconnected by communication links that use protocols to exchange data and share resources. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, the **five network components** are:

1. **Hosts / End Devices** (PCs, mobile phones, servers)
2. **Transmission Media** (copper, fibre-optic, wireless)
3. **Intermediate Devices** (routers, switches, hubs, gateways)
4. **Protocols** (TCP, IP, HTTP, FTP, etc.)
5. **Network Services / Applications** (e-mail, web, VoIP, cloud)

> **Examiner's Note:** Students frequently omit *Protocols* as a separate component. Mark awarded only if all 5 are listed.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Physical and Logical Topology with one example each. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Physical Topology | Logical Topology |
|--------|-------------------|------------------|
| Definition | Actual geometric layout of cables and devices | How data flows, regardless of cables |
| Visibility | You can see it | You can only infer it from traffic |
| Example | Star (cables radiate from a switch) | Ethernet over a star (logically a bus) |
| Example 2 | Ring physical layout | Token Ring data flow (logically a ring) |

**Conclusion:** Physical topology and logical topology are **independent** dimensions. A network can be physically star-shaped and logically bus-shaped (e.g., classic Ethernet).

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Choice A (14 Marks)

### Question A: With a neat diagram, explain the OSI 7-layer reference model. Identify the PDU at each layer and list at least two protocols/devices associated with each layer. [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]:** Draw and explain the **OSI 7-Layer Model** with PDUs and key functions.

**Model Answer:**

The OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) Reference Model was developed by ISO in 1984. It standardizes network communication into **7 functional layers**, each performing a specific role and offering services to the layer above.

| # | Layer | PDU | Key Function | Example Protocols / Devices |
|---|-------|-----|---------------|------------------------------|
| 7 | Application | Message | Network services to the end user | HTTP, FTP, DNS |
| 6 | Presentation | Message | Translation, encryption, compression | SSL/TLS, JPEG, ASCII |
| 5 | Session | Message | Dialogue control, synchronization | NetBIOS, RPC |
| 4 | Transport | Segment / Datagram | End-to-end reliability, port addressing | TCP, UDP |
| 3 | Network | Packet | Logical addressing, routing | IP, ICMP, Router |
| 2 | Data Link | Frame | Framing, MAC addressing, error detection | Ethernet, Switch |
| 1 | Physical | Bit | Bit transmission over medium | Cable, Hub, Repeater |

**Explanation (Top-Down):**

- **Layer 7 (Application):** Closest to the user. Provides the interface for applications like web browsers and email clients to use network services.
- **Layer 6 (Presentation):** Translates data between different formats (e.g., EBCDIC ↔ ASCII), handles encryption/decryption, and compression.
- **Layer 5 (Session):** Establishes, manages, and terminates sessions between two communicating hosts. Provides synchronization via checkpoints.
- **Layer 4 (Transport):** Provides either **connection-oriented** (TCP) or **connectionless** (UDP) delivery. Uses **port numbers** to identify applications. The **Segment** is the PDU for TCP.
- **Layer 3 (Network):** Provides **logical addressing** (IP) and **routing** — the process of determining the best path. **Routers** operate here.
- **Layer 2 (Data Link):** Converts bits into **Frames**, provides **MAC addressing** (48-bit hardware address), and detects errors using **CRC**. **Switches** operate here.
- **Layer 1 (Physical):** Defines electrical, mechanical, and procedural aspects of the physical medium. Transmits raw **bits**. **Hubs and Repeaters** operate here.

**Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]:** Explain **encapsulation and decapsulation** with a neat diagram. Show how a message from the application layer is wrapped as it descends the stack.

**Model Answer:**

**Encapsulation** is the process of **adding a header (and trailer)** at each layer as data moves **down** the stack on the sending side.

**Decapsulation** is the **reverse** — stripping headers/trailers as data moves **up** the stack on the receiving side.

**Step-by-Step Process:**

1. **Application layer** generates the message — call it **Data**.
2. **Transport layer** (TCP) appends a header containing source/destination port → forms a **Segment**.
3. **Network layer** (IP) appends a header with source/destination IP and TTL → forms a **Packet / Datagram**.
4. **Data Link layer** (Ethernet) appends a header (src/dst MAC) and a **CRC trailer** → forms a **Frame**.
5. **Physical layer** transmits the frame as **bits** (voltage pulses or light signals).

At the receiver, the process reverses — bits → frame → packet → segment → message → application.

**ASCII Diagram (KTU Exam-Ready):**

```
Sender                                             Receiver
App Data  →  Seg(TCP)  →  Pkt(IP)  →  Frame(ETH)  →  Bits  →  →  →  →  App Data
   ↑            ↑            ↑            ↑           ↑
   App          TCP          IP        Ethernet    Physical
```

**Conclusion:** The encapsulation/decapsulation model enables each layer to operate independently. A change in one layer (e.g., upgrading Ethernet to Wi-Fi) does not require changes in the others.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question — Choice B (14 Marks) (Internal Choice Alternative)

### Question B: (a) Compare the OSI 7-layer model with the TCP/IP model. State the advantages and disadvantages of each. (b) Explain the various physical topologies used in computer networks with diagrams. [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]:** Compare OSI and TCP/IP models.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | OSI Model | TCP/IP Model |
|---------|-----------|---------------|
| Number of layers | 7 | 4 or 5 |
| Developed by | ISO | DARPA (DoD) |
| Nature | Theoretical reference | Practical implementation |
| Adoption | Universal reference | Foundation of the Internet |
| Session & Presentation | Separate dedicated layers | Combined into Application |
| Network Access layer | Split into Physical and Data Link | Combined into Network Access |
| Protocol dependency | Protocol-independent | Protocol-centric |
| Strict layering | Yes (rigid) | No (flexible) |
| Examples | Conceptual only | TCP, IP, HTTP, FTP |

**Advantages of OSI:**
- Clear, modular structure for teaching and design.
- Strong conceptual separation between functional layers.
- Vendor-neutral reference for interoperability.

**Disadvantages of OSI:**
- Too complex; was never widely implemented.
- Slow standardization; TCP/IP won the protocol war.
- Protocol-agnostic → harder to map to real systems.

**Advantages of TCP/IP:**
- Practical, battle-tested on the global Internet.
- Simpler, with fewer layers to implement.
- Extensible — easy to add new protocols.

**Disadvantages of TCP/IP:**
- Loose layering → can lead to inter-layer dependencies.
- Combined Session/Presentation/Application → less modular.
- Originally designed for US DoD needs; some quirks remain.

**Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]:** Explain physical topologies.

**Model Answer:**

Physical topology defines the geometric layout of a network.

1. **Bus Topology:** All devices share a common backbone cable terminated at both ends. **Advantages:** simple, cheap. **Disadvantages:** single point of failure, collisions.

2. **Star Topology:** All devices connect to a central hub/switch. **Advantages:** robust, easy to install. **Disadvantages:** central device failure paralyzes the network.

3. **Ring Topology:** Each device connects to exactly two neighbors, forming a closed loop. **Advantages:** predictable performance, no collisions (with token). **Disadvantages:** one break disrupts the whole ring (solved by dual ring).

4. **Mesh Topology:** Every device connects to every other (full mesh) or several (partial mesh). **Advantages:** maximum redundancy, fault-tolerant. **Disadvantages:** expensive, complex ($n(n-1)/2$ links).

5. **Tree (Hierarchical) Topology:** A combination of bus and star, organized in levels. **Advantages:** scalable, organized. **Disadvantages:** root failure is catastrophic.

6. **Hybrid Topology:** Mix of any two or more — e.g., star-bus, star-ring.

7. **Point-to-Point Topology:** Simplest, with a dedicated link between two devices.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 1:**
> 1. **PDU Mix-Up (–1 mark):** Many students interchange **Segment** and **Datagram**, or call the Network layer's PDU a "Frame". Memorize: Transport = Segment, Network = Packet/Datagram, Data Link = Frame, Physical = Bit.
> 2. **OSI vs TCP/IP Layer Count (–1 mark):** OSI has 7 layers; TCP/IP has 4 or 5 (use 5 for KTU). Do NOT call TCP/IP a "7-layer" model.
> 3. **Physical vs Logical Topology (–1 mark):** Confusing Ethernet (logical bus) with star (physical) is a frequent oversight. Always state **both** dimensions.
> 4. **Forgetting Protocols/Devices per Layer (–1 mark):** Examiners expect at least 2 protocols OR 1 protocol + 1 device per OSI layer. Just stating the function is not enough.
> 5. **Confusing Hubs/Switches/Routers (–1 mark):** Hub = Layer 1, Switch = Layer 2, Router = Layer 3. This is a guaranteed 1-mark trap.
> 6. **Delay Formulas (–2 marks):** Forgetting to convert units. $R$ in bps and $L$ in bits; if given in KB and Mbps, convert first.
> 7. **Internet Definition:** The Internet is a "**network of networks**" using TCP/IP, **not** "a single network".

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **MODULE 1 — RAPID REVISION**

### A. Core Definitions
- [ ] **Computer Network** = Interconnected autonomous devices using protocols to share resources.
- [ ] **Network Components** = 5: Hosts, Media, Intermediate Devices, Protocols, Services.
- [ ] **Internet** = Global, public, packet-switched network of networks using TCP/IP.
- [ ] **Network Criteria** = Performance, Reliability, Security, Scalability, Fairness.
- [ ] **Bandwidth** = Theoretical max data rate (bps). **Throughput** = Actual achieved rate.

### B. Network Models
- [ ] **OSI** = 7 layers, ISO, theoretical.
- [ ] **TCP/IP** = 4–5 layers, DARPA, practical.
- [ ] **OSI Layers** = App, Pres, Sess, Trans, Net, DLink, Phys (A-P-S-T-N-D-P top-down).
- [ ] **PDUs** = Message → Segment → Packet → Frame → Bit.
- [ ] **Devices per Layer** = Phys (Hub), DLink (Switch), Net (Router).
- [ ] **Encapsulation** = Header added on sender. **Decapsulation** = Header removed on receiver.

### C. Physical Topology
- [ ] **7 Topologies** = Bus, Star, Ring, Mesh, Tree, Hybrid, Point-to-Point.
- [ ] **Full Mesh Links** = $n(n-1)/2$.
- [ ] **Physical ≠ Logical**. Example: Star cables with Ethernet = logical bus.

### D. Key Numerical Formulas
- [ ] **Propagation Delay** $T_{prop} = D/V$, $V \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s in fibre.
- [ ] **Transmission Delay** $T_{trans} = L/R$.
- [ ] **End-to-End Delay** = Sum of all delays across all hops.
- [ ] **Bandwidth-Delay Product** = $R \times T_{prop}$.
- [ ] **RTT** $\approx 2 \times (T_{trans} + T_{prop})$.
- [ ] **IPv4** = 32 bits ($2^{32}$ addresses). **IPv6** = 128 bits ($2^{128}$ addresses).

### E. Mnemonics
- [ ] OSI Top→Bottom: **A P S T N D P** ("All People Seem To Need Data Processing").
- [ ] OSI Bottom→Top: **P D N T S P A** ("Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away").

### F. Most-Asked Topics
- [ ] Draw & explain OSI 7 layers with PDUs (every year).
- [ ] Compare OSI vs TCP/IP (every year).
- [ ] Explain encapsulation/decapsulation.
- [ ] Draw and explain at least 2 topologies.
- [ ] Define Internet and its components.
- [ ] Calculate delay using formulas.

> **Final Word:** Module 1 is the foundation for every later module (Data Link, Network Layer, Transport). A strong grip on **layering, PDUs, and delay formulas** will fetch marks throughout the semester.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical Layer: Transmission Media (Copper, Fiber, Wireless), Datagram Networks, Virtual Circuit Networks, Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST724 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Physical Layer: Transmission Media (Copper, Fiber, Wireless), Datagram Networks, Virtual Circuit Networks, Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Physical Layer — Formal KTU Definition

The **Physical Layer** is the lowest layer of the **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection)** and **TCP/IP** reference models. It is responsible for the **bit-level transmission, reception, and physical encoding** of raw binary data over a communication channel. Its sole concerns are **mechanical** (connectors, cables), **electrical** (voltage levels, impedance), **functional** (bit synchronization, signal encoding), and **procedural** (activation/deactivation of physical links) characteristics of the network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The Physical Layer transports raw bits across a transmission medium. It does **not** interpret frames, packets, or addresses — that responsibility belongs to the **Data Link Layer** (Layer 2) and above. The KTU examiner will award **0 marks** for any answer that conflates Physical Layer duties with Data Link Layer duties (e.g., MAC addressing, error detection).

### Conceptual Analogy — The Postal System

Imagine you are mailing a sealed letter across the country:
- The **letter content** = Application Data (Layer 7)
- The **envelope + address** = Header added by Network Layer (Layer 3)
- The **truck that physically carries the envelope** = The Physical Layer (Layer 1)

The truck does not read the letter, does not validate the address, and does not care if the letter gets wet. It simply transports the **physical signal** from Point A to Point B. The choice of vehicle — a bicycle (copper wire), a high-speed train (fiber optic), or a drone (wireless radio) — is what we call **Transmission Media**.

---

## 1.2 Transmission Media — The Big Three Categories

Transmission media are broadly classified into two families:

| Family | Type | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Guided (Wired)** | Solid medium | Twisted-Pair Copper, Coaxial Cable, Fiber Optic |
| **Unguided (Wireless)** | Electromagnetic spectrum | Radio, Microwave, Satellite, Infrared |

> [!NOTE]
> **Guided media** confine the signal to a physical path (a copper wire or glass fiber). **Unguided media** broadcast the signal into open space (air, vacuum). This single distinction drives every engineering trade-off you will encounter: cost, attenuation, bandwidth, EMI (Electromagnetic Interference), and security.

### 1.2.1 Copper — Twisted-Pair & Coaxial

**Twisted-Pair Cable:** Two insulated copper wires twisted together to **cancel electromagnetic interference (crosstalk)**. Used in **Ethernet (10BASE-T, 100BASE-TX, 1000BASE-T)**, telephone lines, and DSL.

- **UTP (Unshielded Twisted Pair):** Cheap, common, used indoors.
- **STP (Shielded Twisted Pair):** Has a metallic shield; resists EMI better.

**Coaxial Cable:** A central copper conductor surrounded by insulation, a metallic shield, and an outer jacket. Used in **cable television, older Ethernet (10BASE-2/5)**, and long-distance telecom trunk lines before fiber dominance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metric for KTU:** The standard measurement unit for data rate in guided media is **bits per second (bps)**, and the standard frequency measurement is **Hertz (Hz)**. The standard attenuation for UTP is approximately **2-3 dB per 100 m** at 100 MHz.

### 1.2.2 Fiber Optic — The Light-Speed Highway

A **glass or plastic fiber** carries data as **pulses of light** (typically from a laser or LED). Two principal types exist:

- **SMF (Single-Mode Fiber):** Core diameter ≈ **9 µm**, uses a laser, supports very long distances (up to **100 km+** without amplification). Used in undersea cables, ISP backbones.
- **MMF (Multi-Mode Fiber):** Core diameter ≈ **50–62.5 µm**, uses an LED, supports shorter distances (up to **2 km**). Used in LAN backbones, data centers.

Fiber is immune to **EMI**, has extremely low attenuation (**0.2 dB/km** at 1550 nm wavelength), and offers the highest bandwidth commercially available (**100 Gbps and beyond**).

### 1.2.3 Wireless — Electromagnetic Propagation

Wireless channels transmit data using **radio waves, microwaves, or infrared** through the air. Common categories include:

- **Terrestrial Microwave:** Line-of-sight links up to ~50 km, used in cellular backhaul.
- **Satellite Communication:** GEO (35,786 km altitude), MEO, LEO constellations (e.g., Starlink).
- **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11):** Short-range, 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz bands, up to ~100 m.
- **Bluetooth, ZigBee, NFC:** Short-range personal-area networks.

> [!CAUTION]
> Wireless is a **shared, broadcast medium**. Every station in range hears every transmission. This introduces problems like **hidden terminal**, **exposed terminal**, and **multipath fading** — topics covered in the Data Link Layer module. For KTU, remember: **wireless is half-duplex by nature** in most practical implementations.

---

## 1.3 Datagram Networks — The "No-Reservation" Model

A **datagram network** is a **connectionless packet-switching** model. Each packet (datagram) is routed **independently** based on the destination address in its header. There is **no pre-established path**, no handshake, and no guarantee that two consecutive packets from the same sender will follow the same route.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world example:** The **Internet** is the canonical datagram network, built on the **IP (Internet Protocol)**. When you load a webpage, the underlying TCP segments are encapsulated in IP datagrams that may travel through completely different routers on their way to the destination. The network makes **no effort** to maintain state about your connection at Layer 3.

**Analogy:** Throwing individual letters into a postal sorting system. Each letter carries a full address, and the post office sorts each one independently. Some may arrive out of order, some may take different routes.

---

## 1.4 Virtual Circuit Networks — The "Pre-Booked Lane" Model

A **Virtual Circuit (VC) network** is a **connection-oriented packet-switching** model. Before any data flows, a **virtual path (logical circuit)** is established between source and destination through a handshake. All subsequent packets follow this pre-established path, identified by a **Virtual Circuit Identifier (VCI)**.

**Two sub-phases:**
1. **VC Setup:** Source and destination exchange signaling messages to establish the VCI.
2. **Data Transfer:** All packets carry the VCI and follow the same logical path.
3. **VC Teardown:** The circuit is explicitly released.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world example:** **Asynchronous Transfer Mode (ATM)** and **Frame Relay** are classic virtual circuit technologies. **MPLS (Multiprotocol Label Switching)** is a modern hybrid. Note that **TCP** is connection-oriented at Layer 4, but the **IP network** underneath is still datagram-based — a frequently tested distinction in KTU exams.

**Analogy:** Booking a dedicated train compartment. You reserve the path, and every parcel you send uses that same reserved lane. The network keeps state for your connection.

---

## 1.5 Network Performance — What KTU Actually Tests

Performance at the physical and network layer is quantified by these key metrics:

- **Bandwidth (B):** Maximum theoretical data rate of the link, measured in **bits per second (bps)**, **kbps**, **Mbps**, or **Gbps**.
- **Throughput:** The *actual* rate of successful data delivery, always **less than or equal to bandwidth**.
- **Latency (Delay):** Time taken for a bit/packet to travel from source to destination, measured in **seconds (s)** or **milliseconds (ms)**.
- **Round-Trip Time (RTT):** Time for a packet to go from source → destination → back to source.
- **Jitter:** Variation in packet arrival times; critical for voice/video.
- **Packet Loss:** Percentage of packets that fail to reach the destination.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Trap:** Students often confuse **bandwidth** (the width of the pipe) with **throughput** (the actual water that flows). A 100 Mbps link may deliver only 60 Mbps of throughput due to protocol overhead, congestion, or errors. The examiner will mark "bandwidth" as the *theoretical maximum*, not the actual achieved rate.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Guided Media — The Engineering Trade-off Matrix

| Parameter | Twisted-Pair Copper (UTP) | Coaxial Cable | Single-Mode Fiber | Multi-Mode Fiber |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Medium** | Copper wires | Copper + shield | Glass (9 µm core) | Glass (50 µm core) |
| **Typical Bandwidth** | 100 MHz – 500 MHz | ~1 GHz | >10 THz | 200–500 MHz·km |
| **Max Practical Range** | 100 m | 500 m | 100+ km | 2 km |
| **Attenuation** | 2–3 dB/100 m @ 100 MHz | ~1 dB/100 m | 0.2 dB/km | ~1 dB/km |
| **EMI Susceptibility** | High | Moderate | **None** (immune) | **None** (immune) |
| **Security** | Low (tappable) | Low–Medium | **Very High** (hard to tap) | **Very High** |
| **Cost (per meter)** | Very Low | Low | High (plus termination) | Moderate |
| **KTU Typical Use** | LAN Ethernet | Cable TV, older LAN | ISP backbone, undersea | Data center, LAN backbone |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does fiber beat copper?** Three physical reasons — (1) light has a much higher carrier frequency (~10¹⁴ Hz) than electrical signals (~10⁹ Hz), enabling far higher modulation rates; (2) glass has vastly lower attenuation than copper; (3) light is unaffected by electromagnetic interference. This is why **all modern long-distance communication is fiber-based**.

---

## 2.2 Wireless Propagation — The Inverse-Square Law

In free space, a radio signal's power density falls off as the **square of the distance** from the transmitter. This is known as **Friis Transmission Equation**:

$$
P_r(d) \;=\; \frac{P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \lambda^2}{(4\pi)^2 \, d^2 \, L}
$$

Where:
- $P_r(d)$ = received power at distance $d$ (Watts)
- $P_t$ = transmitted power (Watts)
- $G_t, G_r$ = antenna gains of transmitter and receiver (dimensionless)
- $\lambda$ = wavelength of the signal (meters)
- $d$ = distance between antennas (meters)
- $L$ = system loss factor (≥ 1)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU: Doubling the distance reduces received power to **one-quarter**. This is the fundamental reason why cellular towers use **smaller cells** in dense urban areas — to keep $d$ small and maintain signal strength.

**Wireless propagation impairments include:**
- **Path Loss:** Signal weakening over distance (Friis above).
- **Shadowing:** Blockage by buildings, terrain (large-scale fading).
- **Multipath Fading:** Constructive/destructive interference from reflected signals (small-scale fading).
- **Noise:** Thermal noise, interference from other transmitters.
- **Atmospheric Absorption:** Rain, fog, humidity absorb RF energy, especially above 10 GHz.

---

## 2.3 Datagram vs. Virtual Circuit — Detailed Operational Comparison

| Property | Datagram Network | Virtual Circuit Network |
|---|---|---|
| **Connection Setup** | Not required | Required (signaling phase) |
| **State at Routers** | **Stateless** (no per-flow info) | **Stateful** (VCI table per router) |
| **Path Selection** | Per-packet, independent | Per-connection, fixed for the duration |
| **Packet Ordering** | Out-of-order possible | In-order guaranteed along the VC |
| **Failure Recovery** | Natural — re-route per packet | Must tear down + re-establish VC |
| **Header Overhead** | Full source + destination address per packet | Small VCI per packet |
| **Resource Reservation** | None — best-effort | Possible (e.g., ATM CBR, VBR) |
| **Real-World Example** | **IP / Internet** | **ATM, Frame Relay, X.25, MPLS** |
| **Suitability** | Bursty traffic, simple network core | Steady traffic, QoS guarantees |

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Frequently Confused Pairs:**
> - **Datagram (Layer 3) ≠ UDP (Layer 4).** UDP is a connectionless *transport* protocol that runs over IP. Datagram is the *network-layer* switching model.
> - **Virtual Circuit (Layer 3) ≠ TCP (Layer 4).** TCP is connection-oriented at Layer 4, but the underlying IP network is still datagram. The "connection" in TCP is a software construct maintained at the end hosts only.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Critical for Numerical Questions)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations. Every KTU ESE question on Module 1 networks is built from this exact set.**

| # | Formula | Variables | Units | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = 2B \log_2(M)$ | $C$ = capacity, $B$ = bandwidth, $M$ = signal levels | bps | **Nyquist** (noiseless channel) |
| 2 | $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | $C$ = capacity, $B$ = bandwidth, $\text{SNR} = S/N$ | bps | **Shannon-Hartley** (noisy channel) |
| 3 | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(S/N)$ | $S$ = signal power, $N$ = noise power | dB | Convert linear SNR to dB |
| 4 | $d_{\text{prop}} = d \big/ s$ | $d$ = physical distance, $s$ = propagation speed | s | Propagation delay |
| 5 | $d_{\text{trans}} = L \big/ R$ | $L$ = packet length, $R$ = link rate | s | Transmission delay |
| 6 | $d_{\text{end}} = N \cdot (L/R) + d \big/ s$ | $N$ = number of links, $L$ = packet bits, $R$ = bit rate, $d$ = distance, $s$ = speed | s | End-to-end delay (store-and-forward) |
| 7 | $\text{Throughput} = \min(R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_N)$ | $R_i$ = rate of link $i$ | bps | Bottleneck link throughput |
| 8 | $\text{BDP} = B \times \text{RTT}$ | $B$ = bandwidth, $\text{RTT}$ = round-trip time | bits | Bandwidth-Delay Product (window sizing) |
| 9 | $s = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s (copper/fiber), $3 \times 10^8$ m/s (vacuum) | — | m/s | Signal propagation speed |
| 10 | $P_r(d) = \dfrac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2}{(4\pi)^2 d^2 L}$ | See Section 2.2 | W | Friis free-space path loss |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The vertical bar symbol $\vert$ in formulas like $L \big/ R$ or $C \big/ 2B$ means **division** here, not absolute value. In **markdown tables**, never type `|` inside cells — it breaks the table parser. Always use $\big/$ for division inside a formula table.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Shannon-Hartley** dictates the **maximum achievable data rate** for any noisy channel; engineers design modulation/coding schemes to approach this limit (e.g., 5G uses up to **1024-QAM**, getting close to Shannon capacity).
- **Propagation delay** is critical for **satellite links** (GEO round trip ~**240 ms** minimum) — the reason why TCP over satellite needs special tuning.
- **Bandwidth-Delay Product** determines the **TCP window size** needed to fully utilize a link (e.g., a 1 Gbps transcontinental link needs a window of $\approx$ 60 MB to keep the pipe full).
- **Datagram routing** is the reason the Internet survived the **ARPANET → Internet** transition — it is **robust** to router/link failure, whereas VC networks required explicit rerouting.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Shannon-Hartley Theorem (Noisy Channel Capacity)

**Statement:** The maximum error-free data rate $C$ (in bps) over a channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz with signal-to-noise ratio $\text{SNR}$ is:

$$
C \;=\; B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})
$$

### Step 1 — Start with the Information-Theoretic Foundation

Claude Shannon proved in 1948 that if a signal is composed of $M$ discrete levels, the number of bits per signal is $\log_2(M)$ bits. The **Nyquist rate** for a noiseless channel gives:

$$
C_{\text{Nyquist}} \;=\; 2B \log_2(M) \quad \text{(bits per second)}
$$

This is the absolute upper bound **only in the absence of noise**.

### Step 2 — Introduce Noise

In a real channel, noise limits how many distinct signal levels can be reliably distinguished. With signal power $S$ and noise power $N$, the maximum number of distinguishable levels is:

$$
M_{\max} \;=\; \sqrt{1 + \frac{S}{N}} \;=\; \sqrt{1 + \text{SNR}}
$$

(The factor of 2 inside the square root comes from the fact that the noise adds equally to the positive and negative excursions of the signal.)

### Step 3 — Substitute $M_{\max}$ into the Nyquist Equation

$$
C \;=\; 2B \log_2\!\Big(\sqrt{1 + \text{SNR}}\,\Big)
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Logarithm Identity $\log_b(x^a) = a \log_b(x)$

Since $\sqrt{1 + \text{SNR}} = (1 + \text{SNR})^{1/2}$:

$$
C \;=\; 2B \cdot \frac{1}{2} \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})
$$

### Step 5 — Simplify

$$
C \;=\; B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) \quad \blacksquare
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Shannon's formula shows that **doubling the bandwidth doubles the capacity**, but **doubling the SNR does NOT double the capacity** — capacity grows only **logarithmically** with SNR. This is why engineers invest heavily in bandwidth (wider spectrum, higher frequency bands) rather than raw power.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — End-to-End Delay Calculation

> **Problem (KTU ESE Style):** A packet of length $L = 8000$ bits is sent over a path of $N = 4$ links, each of rate $R = 2$ Mbps. The physical distance of each link is $d = 2000$ km, and the propagation speed is $s = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s (typical for fiber). Assuming **store-and-forward** switching, compute the **end-to-end delay** for the packet to reach the destination. Ignore queuing and processing delays.

### Step 1 — Identify the Components of End-to-End Delay

In a store-and-forward network with $N$ links, the total delay is:

$$
d_{\text{end-to-end}} \;=\; N \cdot d_{\text{trans}} + d_{\text{prop, total}}
$$

Where:
- $d_{\text{trans}} = L \big/ R$ (transmission delay per link)
- $d_{\text{prop, total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} d_i \big/ s_i$ (sum of propagation delays over all links)

### Step 2 — Compute Transmission Delay per Link

$$
d_{\text{trans}} \;=\; \frac{L}{R} \;=\; \frac{8000 \text{ bits}}{2 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/s}} \;=\; 4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \;=\; 4 \text{ ms}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Propagation Delay per Link

Each link is $d = 2000 \text{ km} = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ m}$:

$$
d_{\text{prop}} \;=\; \frac{d}{s} \;=\; \frac{2 \times 10^6 \text{ m}}{2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}} \;=\; 10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \;=\; 10 \text{ ms}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Total Transmission Delay (All Links)

Because the packet must be fully received before being forwarded to the next link, **all $N$ transmission delays add up**:

$$
N \cdot d_{\text{trans}} \;=\; 4 \times 4 \text{ ms} \;=\; 16 \text{ ms}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute Total Propagation Delay (All Links)

$$
\sum d_{\text{prop}} \;=\; 4 \times 10 \text{ ms} \;=\; 40 \text{ ms}
$$

### Step 6 — Total End-to-End Delay

$$
d_{\text{end-to-end}} \;=\; 16 \text{ ms} + 40 \text{ ms} \;=\; 56 \text{ ms}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** With a 56 ms total delay over 8000 km, the effective throughput for a single packet is $L \big/ d_{\text{end-to-end}} = 8000 \big/ 0.056 \approx 143$ kbps — far less than the 2 Mbps link rate. This illustrates why **pipelining** (sending back-to-back packets) is essential to approach link capacity.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Shannon Capacity

> **Problem:** A channel has bandwidth $B = 4$ kHz and a signal-to-noise ratio of $\text{SNR} = 30$ dB. Find the maximum data rate.

### Step 1 — Convert SNR from dB to Linear Scale

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} \;=\; 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)
$$

$$
30 \;=\; 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{S}{N}\right) \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{S}{N} \;=\; 10^{30/10} \;=\; 10^3 \;=\; 1000
$$

### Step 2 — Apply Shannon's Formula

$$
C \;=\; B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) \;=\; 4000 \times \log_2(1 + 1000)
$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate $\log_2(1001)$

$$
\log_2(1001) \;=\; \frac{\log_{10}(1001)}{\log_{10}(2)} \;\approx\; \frac{3.0004}{0.3010} \;\approx\; 9.967
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Capacity

$$
C \;\approx\; 4000 \times 9.967 \;\approx\; 39{,}868 \text{ bps} \;\approx\; 39.87 \text{ kbps}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modem History Note:** This is exactly the regime that telephone-line modems (V.34 at 33.6 kbps, V.90 at 56 kbps) operated in during the 1990s — a 4 kHz voice channel with ~30 dB SNR, achieving data rates within 70% of the Shannon limit.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Switching Paradigms

| Decision Criterion | Choose Datagram When... | Choose Virtual Circuit When... |
|---|---|---|
| Traffic pattern | **Bursty**, unpredictable (web browsing, email) | **Steady, continuous** (voice, video streaming) |
| QoS requirement | Best-effort is acceptable | **Strict** delay/jitter bounds required |
| Network scale | **Very large** (millions of nodes) | **Smaller, managed** networks (telco, ATM) |
| Router complexity | **Low** (no per-flow state) | **High** (VCI tables must be maintained) |
| Failure tolerance | **High** (re-route per packet) | **Low** (must re-establish VC) |
| Header overhead | **High** (full address per packet) | **Low** (small VCI per packet) |
| Billing model | Flat-rate or volume-based | Connection-time based |
