## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**
**Define congestion. How does it differ from flow control?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Definition (1 mark):** Congestion is a network state in which the volume of data offered to the network exceeds its carrying capacity, leading to packet loss, increased delay, and reduced throughput.
2. **Scope difference (1 mark):** Flow control operates *end-to-end* between sender and receiver to prevent the receiver's buffer from overflowing; congestion control operates between sender and *network* to prevent network resource overload.
3. **Signaling mechanism (1 mark):** Flow control uses the receiver's advertised window (`rwnd`); congestion control uses the sender's congestion window (`cwnd`) adjusted by algorithms like AIMD.

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**List any three differences between open-loop and closed-loop congestion control.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **Feedback:** Open-loop uses *no feedback*; closed-loop *requires* feedback.
2. **Timing:** Open-loop acts *before* congestion occurs (preventive); closed-loop acts *after* (reactive).
3. **Adaptability:** Open-loop is *static / pre-planned*; closed-loop is *dynamic / adaptive*.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question B1 (Option 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **Leaky Bucket** and **Token Bucket** algorithms for traffic shaping. Compare their burst-handling characteristics. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **TCP Slow Start** and **Congestion Avoidance** mechanisms with their transition rules and growth laws. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — Leaky Bucket vs Token Bucket

**Step 1 — Leaky Bucket (3 marks):**
- A bucket of capacity $B$ packets drains at constant rate $\rho$ packets/sec.
- Input may be bursty; output is always uniform.
- If bucket fills, excess packets are **dropped** (or marked).
- Acts as a *traffic policer* — enforces strict output rate.
- **Formula:** Output rate $\rho$ is independent of input. Maximum queue length $= B$.

**Step 2 — Token Bucket (3 marks):**
- A bucket holds up to $b$ tokens, generated at rate $r$ tokens/sec.
- A packet of size $L$ bytes requires $L/t$ tokens (where $t$ is tokens per byte).
- If insufficient tokens, packet must **wait** (or is dropped).
- Allows **controlled bursts** up to bucket depth $b$.
- **Maximum burst:** $b + r \cdot t_{\text{elapsed}}$.

**Step 3 — Comparison (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Leaky Bucket | Token Bucket |
|---|---|---|
| Output rate | Constant | Variable (up to capacity) |
| Burst handling | Eliminates bursts | Allows controlled bursts |
| Output buffer | Always full possible | May be empty |
| Use case | Strict policing (ATM) | Shaping (IP networks) |

#### Model Solution to (b) — Slow Start & Congestion Avoidance

**Step 1 — Slow Start (3.5 marks):**
- **Initial state:** $\text{cwnd} = 1$ MSS.
- **Per ACK:** $\text{cwnd} \gets \text{cwnd} + 1\ \text{MSS}$.
- **Per RTT:** $\text{cwnd}$ **doubles** (exponential growth).
- **Termination:**
  - $\text{cwnd} \ge \text{ssthresh}$ → switch to Congestion Avoidance.
  - Loss detected → trigger Fast Retransmit / Timeout.
- **Purpose:** Quickly probe available bandwidth.

**Step 2 — Congestion Avoidance (3.5 marks):**
- **Entry:** When $\text{cwnd} \ge \text{ssthresh}$.
- **Per ACK:** $\text{cwnd} \gets \text{cwnd} + \dfrac{\text{MSS}^2}{\text{cwnd}}$ (in bytes).
- **Per RTT:** $\text{cwnd} \gets \text{cwnd} + 1\ \text{MSS}$ (linear growth).
- **Termination:** Loss event (timeout or triple-dup-ACK).
- **Purpose:** Carefully probe for extra bandwidth near the bottleneck without overshooting.

---

### Question B1 (Option 2 — Internal Choice) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain **closed-loop congestion control** with its three operational phases. Mention two techniques in each phase. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A TCP Reno connection starts with $\text{cwnd} = 1$ MSS and $\text{ssthresh} = 8$ MSS. Trace the value of cwnd and ssthresh for the next **10 RTTs**, assuming the connection experiences **a timeout at the end of RTT 4** and **a triple-duplicate-ACK at the end of RTT 8**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — Closed-Loop Phases

**Phase 1 — Detection (2 marks):**
- Network monitors indicators such as **queue length**, **packet delay**, **link utilization**.
- Techniques: (i) **Average queue length threshold**, (ii) **Packet loss observation** by source (implicit).

**Phase 2 — Feedback (2.5 marks):**
- Information about congestion delivered to source.
- Techniques: (i) **Choke packets** (backward signaling), (ii) **ECN** (explicit congestion notification in IP/TCP headers).

**Phase 3 — Adjustment (2.5 marks):**
- Source modifies its sending rate.
- Techniques: (i) **Reduce window** (e.g., TCP cwnd halving on dup-ACK), (ii) **Decrease inter-packet gap**.

#### Model Solution to (b) — Trace Table

**Step 1 — Slow Start Phase (RTTs 1–3):** Doubling per RTT.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RTT 1:} \quad \text{cwnd} &= 2\ \text{MSS} \\
\text{RTT 2:} \quad \text{cwnd} &= 4\ \text{MSS} \\
\text{RTT 3:} \quad \text{cwnd} &= 8\ \text{MSS}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Timeout at RTT 4 (current cwnd = 8):**

$$
\text{ssthresh} = \lfloor 8/2 \rfloor = 4\ \text{MSS}, \quad \text{cwnd} = 1\ \text{MSS}
$$

**Step 3 — Slow Start again (RTTs 5–6):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RTT 5:} \quad \text{cwnd} &= 2\ \text{MSS} \\
\text{RTT 6:} \quad \text{cwnd} &= 4\ \text{MSS} = \text{ssthresh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Congestion Avoidance (RTTs 7–8):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RTT 7:} \quad \text{cwnd} &= 5\ \text{MSS} \\
\text{RTT 8:} \quad \text{cwnd} &= 6\ \text{MSS}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Triple-Dup-ACK at RTT 8 (current cwnd = 6):**

$$
\text{ssthresh} = \lfloor 6/2 \rfloor = 3\ \text{MSS}, \quad \text{cwnd} = 3\ \text{MSS}
$$

(Then continues Congestion Avoidance from cwnd = 3.)

#### Final Summary Table

| RTT | cwnd (MSS) | ssthresh (MSS) | Phase |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1 | 8 | Initial |
| 1 | 2 | 8 | Slow Start |
| 2 | 4 | 8 | Slow Start |
| 3 | 8 | 8 | Slow Start (boundary) |
| 4 | 8 | 8 | Cong. Avoid. start |
| After RTT 4 Timeout | **1** | **4** | Reset |
| 5 | 2 | 4 | Slow Start |
| 6 | 4 | 4 | Slow Start (boundary) |
| 7 | 5 | 4 | Cong. Avoid. |
| 8 | 6 | 4 | Cong. Avoid. |
| After RTT 8 Triple-Dup-ACK | **3** | **3** | Fast Recovery |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing cwnd units:** Many students write cwnd in *bytes* but treat it as *MSS* during halving operations. Always state the unit at every step.
> 2. **Missing the cwnd/ssthresh relationship:** When a loss event occurs, **BOTH** variables update. Writing only `cwnd = 1` without the new `ssthresh = cwnd/2` will cost 2 marks.
> 3. **Misclassifying Reno vs Tahoe behavior:** Reno sets `cwnd = ssthresh` (not 1) on triple-dup-ACK. Tahoe always sets `cwnd = 1`. Examiners frequently set a 2-mark trap on this.
> 4. **Forgetting to state units in formulas:** Writing `cwnd = cwnd + 1` without MSS gets 0.5 mark instead of 1.
> 5. **Skipping the ACK-level vs RTT-level distinction:** Slow Start grows linearly *per ACK* but exponentially *per RTT*. Marking this explicitly earns 1 full mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pre-Exam Bullet Bank — Read Just Before Entering the Hall.**

### Core Concepts
- [ ] **Congestion** = demand for resources exceeds supply in a packet-switched network.
- [ ] **Open-Loop** = preventive, no feedback, decisions pre-planned (Leaky/Token bucket, Admission Control).
- [ ] **Closed-Loop** = reactive, feedback-driven, three phases: detect → inform → adjust.

### TCP Mechanism Highlights
- [ ] $\text{cwnd}$ starts at **1 MSS**, $\text{ssthresh}$ at a high default (typically **64 KB**).
- [ ] **Slow Start:** exponential, $\text{cwnd}$ doubles per RTT.
- [ ] **Congestion Avoidance:** linear, $\text{cwnd}$ grows by 1 MSS per RTT.
- [ ] **Triple-Dup-ACK** (Reno): $\text{ssthresh} = \text{cwnd}/2$, $\text{cwnd} = \text{ssthresh}$, stay in Congestion Avoidance.
- [ ] **Timeout:** $\text{ssthresh} = \text{cwnd}/2$, $\text{cwnd} = 1$, restart Slow Start.
- [ ] **Tahoe vs Reno:** Tahoe always returns to Slow Start; Reno uses Fast Recovery.
- [ ] **AIMD** = Additive Increase (1 MSS/RTT) + Multiplicative Decrease ($\times 1/2$).
- [ ] **Effective window:** $W_{\text{eff}} = \min(\text{rwnd}, \text{cwnd})$.

### Key Formulas (Memorize)
- [ ] Slow Start per ACK: $\text{cwnd} \gets \text{cwnd} + 1$ MSS.
- [ ] Congestion Avoidance per ACK: $\text{cwnd} \gets \text{cwnd} + \text{MSS}^2 / \text{cwnd}$.
- [ ] Loss response: $\text{ssthresh} \gets \lfloor \text{cwnd}/2 \rfloor$.
- [ ] TCP throughput: $B \approx \dfrac{1.22 \cdot \text{MSS}}{\text{RTT} \cdot \sqrt{p}}$.
- [ ] Bandwidth-Delay Product: $\text{BDP} = B \times \text{RTT}$.

### Mnemonics
- [ ] **"SAINT"** for TCP congestion phases: **S**low Start → (boundary at **ssthresh**) → **A**voidance → loss → **I**ncrease/Decrease → (cwnd = cwnd/2) → back to S.
- [ ] **"Pre-plan vs React"** for Open vs Closed loop.

### Common KTU Question Triggers
- [ ] *"Explain slow start with cwnd trace"* — expect a RTT-by-RTT table.
- [ ] *"Differentiate open and closed loop"* — expect a 5-row table with feedback and timing.
- [ ] *"What is AIMD?"* — define additive increase, multiplicative decrease; mention Chiu-Jain fairness.
- [ ] *"Tahoe vs Reno"* — focus on Fast Recovery.
- [ ] *"Throughput under loss"* — apply the $1.22$ formula.

### One-Liners for Each Definition
- [ ] **Congestion Window:** A sender-side limit on in-flight bytes used for congestion control.
- [ ] **ssthresh:** Threshold separating exponential and linear cwnd growth.
- [ ] **Fast Retransmit:** Retransmission triggered by 3 duplicate ACKs without waiting for timeout.
- [ ] **Fast Recovery:** Halving cwnd and skipping Slow Start after Fast Retransmit (Reno only).
- [ ] **ECN:** Explicit Congestion Notification — router marks packet, receiver echoes to sender.
- [ ] **AIMD:** Additive Increase Multiplicative Decrease — fundamental TCP fairness mechanism.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application Layer - Application Layer Paradigms, Client-Server Applications, World Wide Web and HTTP, FTP, Electronic Mail, DNS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST724 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Application Layer - Application Layer Paradigms, Client-Server Applications, World Wide Web and HTTP, FTP, Electronic Mail, DNS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Application Layer — Formal Definition

In the **TCP/IP Reference Model**, the **Application Layer** is the topmost layer (Layer 7 in the OSI model) that provides **network services directly to end-user applications and processes**. It is the only layer through which human users (or software agents) directly interact with the network stack. Unlike the lower layers, it does **not** provide services to any other layer; it consumes the services of the Transport Layer (TCP/UDP) below it.

Per the **KTU 2024 OECST724 syllabus**, the Application Layer is studied through the lens of:
- **Application Layer Paradigms** (architectural patterns)
- **Client-Server Applications**
- **World Wide Web (WWW) and HTTP**
- **File Transfer Protocol (FTP)**
- **Electronic Mail (SMTP, POP3, IMAP)**
- **Domain Name System (DNS)**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Emphasis (2024 Scheme):** The Application Layer is the most *application-oriented* layer. KTU questions frequently test the difference between **connection-oriented (TCP-based)** and **connectionless (UDP-based)** services at this layer, along with the **port numbers** reserved for each protocol.

### 1.2 Application Layer Paradigms — The Two Architectures

A **paradigm** here refers to the architectural pattern that governs how two application processes communicate over a network. KTU focuses on two dominant paradigms:

#### A. Client–Server Paradigm
A **client** process initiates contact with a **server** process. The server is a always-on host with a **well-known, fixed IP address** that waits passively for client requests. Clients may have dynamic IP addresses and communicate intermittently.

#### B. Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Paradigm
There is **no dedicated server**. Peers (arbitrary end-systems) communicate directly with each other, and any peer can act as both client and server. Examples: BitTorrent, Gnutella, Skype (early versions).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** The Client–Server model is **scalability-limited** because the server is a single point of failure and a bandwidth bottleneck. The P2P model exhibits **excellent self-scalability** because each peer adds both demand *and* service capacity.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant vs. The Potluck

- **Client–Server is like a Restaurant 🍽️:** One chef (the server) takes orders from many diners (clients). The chef is always present; the diners come and go. The chef is the bottleneck.
- **Peer-to-Peer is like a Potluck Dinner 🎉:** Every guest brings a dish and shares it. The more guests that come, the more food there is. No single host is overwhelmed.

### 1.4 The Standard Client–Server Application Template

A typical client–server application has two components:
1. **Application-Layer Protocol** — defines the *syntax* (format) and *semantics* (meaning) of exchanged messages, and the *rules* (ordering) of communication (e.g., HTTP, SMTP).
2. **Process / Socket Interface** — the *API* through which the application uses the underlying transport (TCP/UDP) services.

### 1.5 The Three Standard Network Applications (KTU Focus Areas)

| Application | Protocol(s) | Transport | Default Port(s) | Function |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Web (WWW)** | HTTP / HTTPS | TCP | **80** / **443** | Fetching web pages |
| **File Transfer** | FTP (control + data) | TCP | **21** (control), **20** (data) | Bulk file transfer |
| **E-mail** | SMTP, POP3, IMAP | TCP | **25**, **110**, **143** | Sending/Receiving mail |
| **Name Resolution** | DNS | UDP (mostly) | **53** | Translating names to IPs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bolded constants to memorize for KTU exams:** Port **80** (HTTP), **443** (HTTPS), **21** (FTP-control), **20** (FTP-data), **25** (SMTP), **110** (POP3), **143** (IMAP), **53** (DNS).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Process Communication — Sockets and Addressing

For two processes to communicate across a network, they need:
- **IP Address** of the destination host (32-bit for IPv4, 128-bit for IPv6).
- **Port Number** — a 16-bit integer (range **0 to 65535**) that identifies the *specific process* on that host.

The combination `<IP Address, Port Number>` uniquely identifies an endpoint, called a **Socket**.

### 2.2 Transport Service Demands of Common Applications

| Application | Data Loss Tolerance | Bandwidth | Time-Sensitive? | Preferred Transport |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **File Transfer (FTP)** | No loss | Elastic | No | **TCP** |
| **Web (HTTP)** | No loss | Elastic | No (mostly) | **TCP** |
| **E-mail (SMTP)** | No loss | Elastic | No | **TCP** |
| **DNS Query** | Tolerates loss | Low | **Yes** | **UDP** (with TCP fallback) |
| **Streaming Video** | Tolerates loss | High | **Yes** | **UDP** |

### 2.3 World Wide Web (WWW) and HTTP

**HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol)** is the application-layer protocol that forms the backbone of the WWW. Defined originally in **RFC 1945 (HTTP/1.0)** and **RFC 2616 / RFC 7230 (HTTP/1.1)**, and later **RFC 7540 (HTTP/2)** and **RFC 9110 (HTTP/3)**.

**Key Characteristics:**
- HTTP is a **stateless protocol** — the server maintains no information about past client requests.
- Uses **TCP** as the underlying transport (port **80** for HTTP, **443** for HTTPS).
- A web page consists of a **base HTML file** and several **referenced objects** (images, CSS, JS).

**Two Connection Models:**
- **Non-Persistent HTTP (HTTP/1.0):** A separate TCP connection is opened for *each* object. Up to $n+1$ TCP connections for a page with $n$ referenced objects.
- **Persistent HTTP (HTTP/1.1 default):** A single TCP connection is reused for *all* objects, with pipelining support. Default: 1 connection for $n+1$ objects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Trick:** Students often confuse *round-trip time* with *response time*. The response time for a non-persistent HTTP page with $n$ objects is calculated carefully below.

### 2.4 HTTP Message Formats

**HTTP Request Message (ASCII text):**
```
GET /index.html HTTP/1.1\r\n
Host: www.example.com\r\n
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0\r\n
Accept: text/html\r\n
Connection: keep-alive\r\n
\r\n
```

**HTTP Response Message (ASCII text):**
```
HTTP/1.1 200 OK\r\n
Date: Mon, 23 May 2005 22:38:34 GMT\r\n
Server: Apache/2.0\r\n
Content-Length: 438\r\n
Content-Type: text/html\r\n
\r\n
<html>... </html>
```

### 2.5 Common HTTP Status Codes (KTU-Favorite)

| Code | Meaning | Category |
|---|---|---|
| **200** | OK | Success |
| **301** | Moved Permanently | Redirection |
| **400** | Bad Request | Client Error |
| **404** | Not Found | Client Error |
| **505** | HTTP Version Not Supported | Server Error |

### 2.6 File Transfer Protocol (FTP)

FTP is a stateful, **connection-oriented** file transfer protocol. It uses **two parallel TCP connections**:
1. **Control Connection (Port 21):** Carries commands (USER, PASS, LIST, RETR, STOR) and replies. Remains open for the entire session.
2. **Data Connection (Port 20):** Carries the actual file data. Opens and closes with each file transfer.

**Two Modes of Data Connection:**
- **Active Mode:** Server initiates the data connection *back* to the client (problematic with NAT/firewalls).
- **Passive Mode:** Client initiates the data connection *to* the server (firewall-friendly).

### 2.7 Electronic Mail (SMTP, POP3, IMAP)

The e-mail system has three major components:
- **User Agents (UA):** Mail readers (Outlook, Gmail, Thunderbird).
- **Mail Servers:** Send, receive, and store mail. Each user has a **mailbox**.
- **Simple Mail Transfer Protocol (SMTP):** The protocol for *pushing* mail from sender's server to receiver's server. Port **25**.

**SMTP characteristics:**
- Uses **TCP**, port **25**.
- **Push protocol** (sender pushes to receiver).
- Uses **7-bit ASCII** (binary data must be encoded, e.g., MIME, Base64).
- Phases: **Handshake → Transfer of messages (MAIL, RCPT, DATA) → Closure**.

**Mail Access Protocols:**
- **POP3 (Post Office Protocol v3), Port 110:** Simple, **stateless across sessions**. Downloads mail by default (destructive). Two modes: *Download-and-delete* and *Download-and-keep*.
- **IMAP (Internet Message Access Protocol), Port 143:** More sophisticated, **maintains state across sessions**. Allows server-side folder management; messages remain on server.

### 2.8 Domain Name System (DNS)

DNS is the **"phone book of the Internet"** — it translates human-friendly **domain names** (e.g., `www.ktu.edu.in`) into machine-friendly **IP addresses** (e.g., `103.42.156.78`).

**Why not centralize DNS?**
- Single point of failure
- Huge traffic volume
- Distant centralized database → high latency
- Maintenance nightmare (every change globally)

**Solution:** A **distributed, hierarchical database**.

**Three-Layer Hierarchy:**
1. **Root DNS Servers** — 13 logical root servers (A through M), with hundreds of physical instances worldwide.
2. **Top-Level Domain (TLD) Servers** — `.com`, `.org`, `.net`, `.edu`, `.gov`, country codes `.in`, `.uk`, etc.
3. **Authoritative DNS Servers** — Provide the actual IP for organizations (e.g., `ktu.edu.in` is served by KTU's authoritative server).

**There is also a fourth concept — Local DNS Server (Resolver):** Each ISP runs one. It is the *first stop* for a host's DNS query.

**DNS Query Types:**
- **Iterative Query:** The contacted server returns the *best answer it has* or refers to another server. The client must do the work.
- **Recursive Query:** The contacted server takes on the burden and returns the *final answer* (or an error).

### 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Socket Identifier** | $\text{Socket} = \langle \text{IP Address}, \text{Port Number} \rangle$ | Port range: $0 \text{ to } 2^{16}-1 = 65535$ |
| **Non-Persistent HTTP Response Time** | $T_{\text{response}} = 2 \cdot RTT + \text{transmission time of base HTML}$ | For first object |
| **Non-Persistent HTTP, page with n objects** | $T_{\text{total}} = (n+1) \cdot (2 \cdot RTT + \text{file transmission})$ | One connection per object |
| **Persistent HTTP (no pipelining)** | $T_{\text{total}} = 1 \cdot (2 \cdot RTT + \text{base}) + n \cdot (RTT + \text{obj})$ | Sequential requests |
| **Persistent HTTP (with pipelining)** | $T_{\text{total}} = 1 \cdot (2 \cdot RTT + \text{base}) + RTT + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{transmit}(obj_i)$ | Back-to-back requests |
| **DNS Resolution Time (Iterative, all cached=none)** | $T_{\text{res}} \approx 4 \cdot RTT$ (Root $\to$ TLD $\to$ Authoritative $\to$ answer) | Typical worst case |
| **DNS Resolution Time (Recursive)** | $T_{\text{res}} \approx RTT_{\text{Local}} + \text{work at servers}$ | Often faster |
| **Packet Transmission Time** | $T_{\text{trans}} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | $L$ = file length (bits), $R$ = bandwidth (bps) |
| **FTP Connections** | $2$ parallel TCP connections per session | $1$ control ($port=21$), $1$ data ($port=20$) |
| **SMTP Phases** | $3$ phases | Handshake $\to$ Transfer $\to$ Closure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Whenever a question mentions "an HTML page and N images", students often forget to add the **base HTML file** to the count. There are always $n+1$ objects, not $n$. This is one of the most common mark-deduction points in KTU 2024 answer sheets.

### 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **HTTP** underpins the entire modern web, REST APIs, and microservices.
- **FTP** is still used in legacy enterprise data exchanges and web hosting deployments.
- **SMTP/POP/IMAP** are the backbones of every email provider (Gmail, Outlook, Yahoo).
- **DNS** is the silent critical infrastructure; an entire DDoS attack on a DNS provider (e.g., the 2016 Dyn attack) can black-out half the Internet.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Problem 1 — Non-Persistent HTTP Response Time

**Problem (KTU-Style):**
A web page consists of one base HTML file and **6 referenced objects** (images). The base HTML is **20 KB** and each object is **15 KB**. The round-trip time (RTT) between the client and the server is **100 ms**. The link bandwidth is **1 Mbps**. Using non-persistent HTTP (no parallel connections), calculate the total response time for the page.

> **Given:**
> - Number of objects: $n = 6$ (so total objects fetched = $n + 1 = 7$)
> - Base HTML size: $L_{\text{base}} = 20 \text{ KB} = 20 \times 8 \times 10^3 \text{ bits} = 160{,}000 \text{ bits}$
> - Each object size: $L_{\text{obj}} = 15 \text{ KB} = 15 \times 8 \times 10^3 \text{ bits} = 120{,}000 \text{ bits}$
> - $RTT = 100 \text{ ms} = 0.1 \text{ s}$
> - Bandwidth $R = 1 \text{ Mbps} = 10^6 \text{ bps}$

**Step 1: Calculate transmission time for the base HTML file.**

$$
T_{\text{trans,base}} = \frac{L_{\text{base}}}{R} = \frac{160{,}000}{10^6} = 0.16 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate transmission time for one referenced object.**

$$
T_{\text{trans,obj}} = \frac{L_{\text{obj}}}{R} = \frac{120{,}000}{10^6} = 0.12 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3: For non-persistent HTTP, EACH object requires a separate TCP connection. The time to fetch ONE object is:**

$$
T_{\text{one}} = 2 \cdot RTT + T_{\text{trans}}
$$

This is because: 1 RTT for TCP handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK — but the *request* is piggybacked on the third segment, so effectively the data arrives 1 RTT after the request goes out, plus another 1 RTT for the initial handshake). Hence, **2 RTTs** for the connection setup + data delivery of the request.

**Step 4: Compute the time to fetch the base HTML.**

$$
T_{\text{base}} = 2 \cdot RTT + T_{\text{trans,base}} = 2(0.1) + 0.16 = 0.20 + 0.16 = 0.36 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the time to fetch each of the 6 objects.**

$$
T_{\text{obj}} = 2 \cdot RTT + T_{\text{trans,obj}} = 2(0.1) + 0.12 = 0.20 + 0.12 = 0.32 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 6: Total time for all 7 objects (sequential, no parallelism).**

$$
T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{base}} + n \cdot T_{\text{obj}} = 0.36 + 6 \times 0.32
$$

$$
T_{\text{total}} = 0.36 + 1.92 = 2.28 \text{ s}
$$

**Final Answer:** $T_{\text{total}} = 2.28 \text{ seconds}$.

### 3.2 Analytical Problem 2 — Persistent HTTP with Pipelining

**Same problem, but using Persistent HTTP with pipelining.**

In persistent HTTP with pipelining, ONE TCP connection is opened for the entire page. The connection takes 1 RTT for the initial TCP handshake. The base HTML is fetched in 1 RTT (request + response). Then, all 6 subsequent object requests are pipelined — they are sent back-to-back, and responses arrive back-to-back. Effectively, only **1 additional RTT** is needed for the *pipeline batch* (since they all go in the same window).

**Step 1: TCP connection setup + base HTML delivery.**

$$
T_{\text{setup+base}} = 1 \cdot RTT + T_{\text{trans,base}} = 0.1 + 0.16 = 0.26 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2: Pipelined request/response for the 6 objects (back-to-back).**

$$
T_{\text{pipeline}} = 1 \cdot RTT + \sum_{i=1}^{6} T_{\text{trans,obj}} = 0.1 + 6 \times 0.12 = 0.1 + 0.72 = 0.82 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3: Total time.**

$$
T_{\text{total,pipeline}} = 0.26 + 0.82 = 1.08 \text{ s}
$$

**Comparison:** Persistent pipelined is **2.28 / 1.08 ≈ 2.11× faster** than non-persistent.

### 3.3 Analytical Problem 3 — DNS Iterative Resolution Time

**Problem:** A host's local DNS resolver does not know the IP for `www.ktu.edu.in`. RTT from host to local DNS is $RTT_1 = 20$ ms. RTT from local DNS to root, TLD, and authoritative servers are each $RTT_2 = 80$ ms. Find the total time for an **iterative** query.

> **Steps of an Iterative Query:**
> 1. Host $\to$ Local DNS (recursive — local DNS MUST do the work): $RTT_1 = 20$ ms.
> 2. Local DNS $\to$ Root server: $RTT_2 = 80$ ms. Root replies: "Ask the .in TLD server."
> 3. Local DNS $\to$ TLD (.in) server: $RTT_2 = 80$ ms. TLD replies: "Ask ktu.edu.in's authoritative server."
> 4. Local DNS $\to$ Authoritative (ktu.edu.in) server: $RTT_2 = 80$ ms. Authoritative replies with the actual IP.
> 5. Local DNS $\to$ Host: $RTT_1 = 20$ ms.

$$
T_{\text{total}} = 2 \cdot RTT_1 + 3 \cdot RTT_2 = 2(20) + 3(80) = 40 + 240 = 280 \text{ ms}
$$

> **Note:** This assumes the time to *transmit* the query/reply is negligible (small DNS packets). For larger replies, the transmission time must be added.

### 3.4 Analytical Problem 4 — Recursive DNS Resolution Time

In a fully recursive query, the local DNS server contacts one root server, which contacts the TLD server, which contacts the authoritative server, in a **chain**. The total time depends on the **chain length** but does NOT multiply RTTs from the host's perspective.

> **Step-by-step:**
> 1. Host $\to$ Local DNS: $RTT_1 = 20$ ms (host asks local DNS).
> 2. Local DNS does the chain (root $\to$ TLD $\to$ auth). All queries/responses are pipelined within the chain; the time is dominated by the sum of RTTs to each server: $3 \cdot RTT_2 = 240$ ms.
> 3. Local DNS $\to$ Host: $RTT_1 = 20$ ms.

$$
T_{\text{recursive}} = 2 \cdot RTT_1 + 3 \cdot RTT_2 = 280 \text{ ms}
$$

> **Why is this still 280 ms?** Because in the iterative case, the local DNS *also* must contact each server. The difference is **who bears the load** (client vs. server). The total time is similar, but **recursive queries are heavier on intermediate servers** (e.g., root servers would melt under recursive load — that's why root servers only support *iterative* queries).

### 3.5 Analytical Problem 5 — FTP Parallel Connections

FTP opens **2 parallel TCP connections** per session. A user logs in, lists a directory, and downloads 3 files. How many TCP connections are opened in total?

- 1 **control connection** (persists for the whole session): 1 connection.
- For each of: LIST + 3 RETRs = 4 data transfer operations: 4 **data connections**.

$$
\text{Total TCP connections} = 1 + 4 = 5
$$

### 3.6 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Protocol Design Choices

| Property | HTTP | FTP | SMTP | DNS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Transport** | TCP | TCP | TCP | UDP (mostly), TCP fallback |
| **Port Number** | 80 / 443 | 21 (control) + 20 (data) | 25 | 53 |
| **Stateful?** | Stateless | Stateful | Stateful | Stateless server |
| **Connection Type** | Persistent / Non-persistent | 2 parallel | Persistent | Single request/reply |
| **Push or Pull** | Pull (client pulls) | Pull (client pulls) | Push (sender pushes) | Pull-then-Push (resolver pulls) |
| **Data Format** | ASCII (request), binary (body) | ASCII (commands), binary (data) | 7-bit ASCII (header), MIME (body) | Binary DNS message format |
| **Authentication** | Optional (Basic/Digest Auth) | Yes (USER/PASS) | Yes (EHLO + AUTH) | No (public service) |
| **Encryption?** | TLS for HTTPS | FTPS / SFTP (separate) | STARTTLS / SMTPS | DNSSEC (signs records) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** If a question asks "Why DNS uses UDP?" — the answer is: **low overhead, fast response for small queries, and most DNS queries are tiny (under 512 bytes)**. For large responses (e.g., DNSSEC, zone transfers), DNS falls back to **TCP** (port 53).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Application Layer Paradigm — Client–Server vs P2P

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph CS["CLIENT SERVER PARADIGM"]
        C1[Client 1] -- Request --> S[Central Server]
        C2[Client 2] -- Request --> S
        C3[Client 3] -- Request --> S
        S -- Response --> C1
        S -- Response --> C2
        S -- Response --> C3
    end
```

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph P2P["PEER TO PEER PARADIGM"]
        P1[Peer A] <-- Direct Exchange --> P2[Peer B]
        P2 <-- Direct Exchange --> P3[Peer C]
        P3 <-- Direct Exchange --> P4[Peer D]
        P4 <-- Direct Exchange --> P1
    end
```

### 4.2 HTTP Transaction Flow (Persistent Connection)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Browser as Web Browser (Client)
    participant Server as Web Server

    Browser->>Server: TCP SYN (Port 80)
    Server-->>Browser: TCP SYN-ACK
    Browser->>Server: TCP ACK (Connection Established)

    Browser->>Server: GET /index.html HTTP/1.1
    Server-->>Browser: HTTP/1.1 200 OK + base HTML

    Browser->>Server: GET /image1.png HTTP/1.1
    Server-->>Browser: 200 OK + image1.png bytes

    Browser->>Server: GET /style.css HTTP/1.1
    Server-->>Browser: 200 OK + style.css bytes

    Browser->>Server: Connection: close
    Server-->>Browser: FIN (Connection Closed)
```

### 4.3 FTP Dual-Channel Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    Client[FTP Client]
    Server[FTP Server]
    Port21[TCP Port 21 - Control Channel]
    Port20[TCP Port 20 - Data Channel]
    CmdOut[Commands: USER, PASS, RETR, STOR, LIST, QUIT]
    FileXfer[Bulk File Data Transfer]

    Client <-->|Control Commands and Replies| Port21
    Port21 <--> Server
    Server <--> Port20
    Port20 <-->|File Bytes Stream| Client
    Port21 -.carries.-> CmdOut
    Port20 -.carries.-> FileXfer
```

### 4.4 E-mail System Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    AliceUA[Alice UA e.g. Outlook]
    AliceMS[Alice Mail Server]
    BobMS[Bob Mail Server]
    BobUA[Bob UA e.g. Gmail Web]

    AliceUA -- SMTP submission port 587 --> AliceMS
    AliceMS -- SMTP push port 25 --> BobMS
    BobMS -- POP3 port 110 OR IMAP port 143 --> BobUA
    BobMS --> BobMailbox[Bob Mailbox on server]
```

> **Reading the flow:**
> 1. **Alice composes** mail in her User Agent.
> 2. Alice's UA **submits** to her mail server (SMTP, port **587**).
> 3. Alice's server **pushes** to Bob's server (SMTP, port **25**).
> 4. Bob's UA **retrieves** from his server using POP3 (port **110**) or IMAP (port **143**).

### 4.5 DNS Hierarchical Resolution (Iterative)

```mermaid
graph TB
    Host[Host wants www.ktu.edu.in]
    Local[Local DNS Resolver at ISP]
    Root[Root DNS Server]
    TLD[.in TLD Server]
    Auth[ktu.edu.in Authoritative Server]

    Host -->|1 Query| Local
    Local -->|2 Query| Root
    Root -->|3 Referral: Ask .in TLD| Local
    Local -->|4 Query| TLD
    TLD -->|5 Referral: Ask ktu.edu.in auth| Local
    Local -->|6 Query| Auth
    Auth -->|7 Answer: 103.42.156.78| Local
    Local -->|8 Final Answer| Host
```

```mermaid
    AppLayer --> P1[Paradigms]
    P1 --> CS[Client Server]
    P1 --> P2P[Peer to Peer]

    AppLayer --> P2[Standard Apps]

    P2 --> FTP[FTP]
    P2 --> Mail[Electronic Mail]
    P2 --> DNS[Domain Name System]

    WWW --> HTTPDetails[HTTP Methods, Status Codes, Persistent vs Non Persistent]
    FTP --> FTPDetails[Control Port 21, Data Port 20, Active vs Passive]
    Mail --> MailDetails[SMTP port 25, POP3 port 110, IMAP port 143]

```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Explain the difference between the Client-Server and Peer-to-Peer paradigms. Give one real-world example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

In the **Client–Server paradigm**, an always-on host (the *server*) with a fixed, well-known IP address provides services to many client hosts. The server is centralized; clients initiate contact. This model suffers from a single point of failure and a scalability bottleneck, but is easier to manage. **Example:** A web server serving web pages to browsers.

In the **Peer-to-Peer (P2P) paradigm**, there is no dedicated server. Peers (arbitrary end-systems) communicate directly, and any peer can act as both client and server. P2P exhibits excellent self-scalability: as more peers join, both demand *and* service capacity increase. **Example:** BitTorrent file sharing.

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**List the well-known port numbers for the following protocols: HTTP, HTTPS, FTP (control), SMTP, DNS, POP3, IMAP.**

**Model Answer:**

| Protocol | Port Number |
|---|---|
| HTTP | **80** |
| HTTPS | **443** |
| FTP (Control) | **21** |
| FTP (Data) | **20** |
| SMTP | **25** |
| DNS | **53** |
| POP3 | **110** |
| IMAP | **143** |

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Module 4]`**
**A web page consists of one base HTML file of 30 KB and 8 referenced image objects, each of size 100 KB. The RTT between the client and server is 200 ms, and the link transmission rate is 2 Mbps. Calculate the total response time to fetch the entire page for the following scenarios: (a) Non-persistent HTTP without parallel connections (7 marks). (b) Persistent HTTP with pipelining (7 marks).**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Base HTML: $L_{\text{base}} = 30 \text{ KB} = 30 \times 8 \times 1024 = 245{,}760 \text{ bits}$
- Each object: $L_{\text{obj}} = 100 \text{ KB} = 100 \times 8 \times 1024 = 819{,}200 \text{ bits}$
- $RTT = 200 \text{ ms} = 0.2 \text{ s}$
- Bandwidth $R = 2 \text{ Mbps} = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}$
- $n = 8$ referenced objects $\Rightarrow$ Total objects = $n + 1 = 9$

**Part (a) — Non-Persistent HTTP (no parallelism):**

**Step 1: Transmission time for the base HTML.**

$$
T_{\text{trans,base}} = \frac{245{,}760}{2 \times 10^6} = 0.12288 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2: Transmission time for one object.**

$$
T_{\text{trans,obj}} = \frac{819{,}200}{2 \times 10^6} = 0.4096 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3: Time to fetch ONE object (including TCP setup).**

$$
T_{\text{one}} = 2 \cdot RTT + T_{\text{trans}} = 2(0.2) + T_{\text{trans}}
$$

For the base HTML:

$$
T_{\text{base}} = 0.4 + 0.12288 = 0.52288 \text{ s}
$$

For each object:

$$
T_{\text{obj}} = 0.4 + 0.4096 = 0.8096 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4: Total time (sequential, 9 separate TCP connections).**

$$
T_{\text{total,non-persistent}} = T_{\text{base}} + 8 \times T_{\text{obj}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{total}} = 0.52288 + 8 \times 0.8096 = 0.52288 + 6.4768 = 6.99968 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{total}} \approx 7.00 \text{ seconds}}
$$

**Part (b) — Persistent HTTP with Pipelining:**

In persistent HTTP with pipelining, ONE TCP connection serves the entire page. The connection setup costs 1 RTT. The base HTML is fetched in 1 RTT. The remaining 8 objects are pipelined — sent back-to-back, costing an additional 1 RTT for the *batch*.

**Step 1: Setup + base HTML.**

$$
T_{\text{setup+base}} = 1 \cdot RTT + T_{\text{trans,base}} = 0.2 + 0.12288 = 0.32288 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2: Pipelined objects (1 RTT for the whole batch + sum of all transmissions).**

$$
T_{\text{pipeline}} = 1 \cdot RTT + 8 \times T_{\text{trans,obj}} = 0.2 + 8 \times 0.4096 = 0.2 + 3.2768 = 3.4768 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3: Total.**

$$
T_{\text{total,pipeline}} = 0.32288 + 3.4768 = 3.79968 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{total,pipeline}} \approx 3.80 \text{ seconds}}
$$

> **Conclusion:** Persistent pipelined HTTP is **~1.84× faster** (7.00 / 3.80) than non-persistent HTTP for this page.

---

#### **Question B `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Module 4 — Alternative Choice]`**
**(a) Explain the working of SMTP with a clear message flow diagram. Why is SMTP called a "push" protocol? Compare SMTP with HTTP. (7 marks)**
**(b) Describe the three-tier hierarchical structure of DNS. Explain iterative and recursive DNS query resolution with an example. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol):**

SMTP is the **application-layer protocol for sending (pushing) e-mail** between mail servers. Defined in **RFC 5321**, it uses **TCP, port 25** (or 587 for submission). It works in three phases:

1. **Handshake (Connection Setup):** Client (sender server) opens a TCP connection to port 25 of the receiver server. Exchange of HELO/EHLO commands identifies the sender.
2. **Message Transfer:** The sender issues:
   - `MAIL FROM:<alice@example.com>` — identifies the sender.
   - `RCPT TO:<bob@example.org>` — identifies the receiver (can be repeated for multiple recipients).
   - `DATA` — followed by the message body terminated by a single period `.` on a line by itself.
3. **Closure:** The sender issues `QUIT`, and the TCP connection is closed.

**SMTP is a "push" protocol** because the *sending* mail server actively pushes the message to the *receiving* mail server. The receiver does not pull.

**Comparison of SMTP and HTTP:**

| Property | HTTP | SMTP |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Pull (client pulls from server) | Push (sender pushes to receiver) |
| **Protocol Action** | Client requests, server responds | Sender transfers, receiver accepts |
| **Object Persistence** | Objects fetched are not stored on server | Messages are *stored* on the receiver's server |
| **State** | Stateless | Stateful during a session |
| **Format of Commands** | ASCII | ASCII |
| **Transport** | TCP, port 80 | TCP, port 25 |
| **Data Encoding** | Multipart MIME supported | 7-bit ASCII; MIME for binary |

**Part (b) — DNS Hierarchy and Query Resolution:**

**Three-Tier Hierarchical Structure of DNS:**

1. **Root DNS Servers** — There are 13 logical root servers worldwide (named A through M, e.g., `a.root-servers.net`). They do not store the actual IP addresses, but know which TLD servers are authoritative for each top-level domain (`.com`, `.org`, `.in`, etc.).
2. **Top-Level Domain (TLD) Servers** — Operated by registries. For each TLD (e.g., `.com` operated by VeriSign; `.in` operated by NIXI), the TLD server knows which *authoritative* server is responsible for each second-level domain (e.g., `ktu.edu.in`).
3. **Authoritative DNS Servers** — Operated by organizations. They provide the actual DNS records (A, AAAA, MX, CNAME, etc.) for their own zones.

A host does not directly contact these servers. It first contacts its **Local DNS Resolver** (run by the ISP), which performs the actual lookups on the host's behalf.

**Iterative Query Resolution:**

The contacted server returns the *best answer it has* — which is often a *referral* to another server. The client (local resolver) must then contact the next server itself.

**Example:** Host asks local resolver for `www.ktu.edu.in`.
1. Local resolver queries **Root** $\to$ Root replies: "I don't know, ask the `.in` TLD server."
2. Local resolver queries **`.in` TLD** $\to$ TLD replies: "I don't know, ask `ktu.edu.in`'s authoritative server."
3. Local resolver queries **Authoritative for `ktu.edu.in`** $\to$ Authoritative replies: "The IP is 103.42.156.78."
4. Local resolver returns the IP to the host.

**Recursive Query Resolution:**

The contacted server takes on the responsibility of resolving the query completely. The client waits for the final answer.

**Example:** Host asks local resolver for `www.ktu.edu.in` *recursively*.
1. Local resolver contacts Root *recursively*. Root contacts TLD. TLD contacts Authoritative. The chain bubbles back the answer to Local, which returns it to the host.
2. Root servers **refuse recursive queries** in practice to protect themselves from overload. They only support *iterative* queries.

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks in This Module:**
> 1. **Forgetting the base HTML file:** When a problem says "an HTML page and N images", you must count $n+1$ objects, not $n$. This is a 1-mark trap.
> 2. **HTTP Timing Formula Mistake:** Students often write $1 \cdot RTT$ instead of $2 \cdot RTT$ for non-persistent HTTP per object. The handshake + request response costs **2 RTTs per object**, not 1.
> 3. **Confusing "stateless" definitions:** HTTP is *stateless* (server forgets client between requests), but FTP and SMTP are *stateful*. Don't mix these up.
> 4. **Mixing up port numbers:** SMTP is **25** for server-to-server transfer and **587** for client submission. POP3 is **110**. IMAP is **143**. Memorize them cold.
> 5. **DNS Server Roles:** Students often say "the root server resolves the domain". This is wrong — the **authoritative server** for the *zone* resolves it. Root servers only provide *referrals*.
> 6. **Persistent vs. Non-persistent HTTP in HTTP/1.1:** HTTP/1.1 defaults to **persistent**, not non-persistent. Students who treat HTTP/1.1 as non-persistent lose marks.
> 7. **Iterative vs. Recursive time:** Many students compute the *same* time but cannot articulate the *trade-off* (iterative = lighter on servers, recursive = lighter on client). Examiners want both.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Application Layer:**

- **Application Layer Paradigms:**
  - **Client–Server:** Centralized server, well-known IP, scalability bottleneck, single point of failure.
  - **Peer-to-Peer (P2P):** No central server, self-scalable, examples: BitTorrent, Gnutella.
  - **Hybrid:** Many real systems are hybrid (e.g., Napster used central index + P2P transfer).

- **Process Addressing:**
  - Endpoints = $\langle$ IP Address, Port Number $\rangle$.
  - Port range: **0–65535**. Well-known ports: **0–1023**.

- **HTTP:**
  - Stateless, **TCP-based**, port **80** (HTTP) / **443** (HTTPS).
  - Non-persistent: **2 RTTs + transmission** per object.
  - Persistent: 1 RTT for setup, then $RTT + \text{transmission}$ for sequential, or 1 RTT for a pipelined batch.
  - Status codes: **200 OK**, **301 Moved**, **400 Bad Request**, **404 Not Found**, **505 Version Not Supported**.

- **FTP:**
  - **2 parallel TCP connections**: control (**21**) and data (**20**).
  - Stateful. Active vs. Passive mode.
  - Commands: **USER, PASS, RETR, STOR, LIST, QUIT**.

- **Electronic Mail:**
  - **SMTP (port 25)** — push, between servers.
  - **POP3 (port 110)** — stateless, downloads mail.
  - **IMAP (port 143)** — stateful, server-side folder management.
  - **MIME** encodes non-ASCII binary content.

- **DNS:**
  - Translates **names to IPs**.
  - **3-tier hierarchy**: **Root $\to$ TLD $\to$ Authoritative**, plus **Local Resolver**.
  - **13 logical root servers** (A–M).
  - **Iterative query** = client does the work. **Recursive query** = server does the work.
  - Primarily uses **UDP, port 53**, with TCP fallback.
  - **DNSSEC** provides authenticated answers.
  - **DNS resource records**: A, AAAA, CNAME, MX, NS, PTR, SOA, TXT.

- **Transport Service Demands:**
  - Loss-intolerant, bandwidth-elastic apps $\to$ **TCP** (FTP, HTTP, SMTP, POP3, IMAP).
  - Loss-tolerant, time-sensitive apps $\to$ **UDP** (DNS, VoIP, video streaming).

- **Engineering Insight:**
  - Persistent HTTP, CDNs, and edge caching are how modern web achieves sub-100 ms responses globally.
  - DNS is the **single most critical** Internet service — its failure takes down everything.
  - SMTP's 7-bit ASCII limitation is why **MIME** (Base64, Quoted-Printable) was invented.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Peer-to-Peer Paradigm - P2P Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST724 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Peer-to-Peer Paradigm - P2P Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Network**
> A **Peer-to-Peer (P2P) network** is a distributed application architecture that partitions tasks or workloads between equally privileged participants (called **peers**). Each peer is simultaneously a *client* (requesting services/resources) and a *server* (providing services/resources) — a model known as the **servent** paradigm (server + client).

In a P2P system, every node in the overlay network both *consumes* and *contributes* resources (bandwidth, storage, CPU cycles), eliminating the need for a central coordinating server to mediate all interactions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The P2P paradigm is one of the **three application architectures** explicitly listed in the KTU Computer Networks syllabus, alongside the *Client-Server model* and the *Hybrid model*. It is a high-weight topic for **ESE Module 4** and frequently carries 7–14 mark questions.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — "The Study Group"

Imagine a classroom of 100 students preparing for the KTU exam:

| Model | How it Works | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Client-Server** | One student (the "topper") makes 100 photocopies of notes and hands them out one by one. | A central office (e.g., college website) hosting all files |
| **Peer-to-Peer** | Each student who already has the notes starts making copies for 2–3 others; soon, the network of students self-propagates the notes. | BitTorrent swarms, file-sharing networks |

The moment a peer finishes downloading, it becomes a *mini-server* that helps distribute the file further. This is the **core magic** of P2P — the system gets *faster* as more users join, opposite to a client-server model which gets *slower*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics for KTU:**
> - **Peer (Node):** An end-host that acts as both client and server.
> - **Overlay Network:** A virtual logical network built on top of the physical Internet.
> - **DHT (Distributed Hash Table):** Decentralized lookup service used in structured P2P.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture Spectrum

KTU examiners expect students to clearly differentiate the three architectures. Let's break them down step-by-step.

### 2.1.1 Client-Server Architecture
- **Fixed roles:** Dedicated server, multiple clients.
- **Flow:** Client always initiates → server always responds.
- **Examples:** HTTP (web), FTP, SMTP (email), DNS.
- **Drawback:** Server is a **single point of failure**; scalability bottleneck.

### 2.1.2 Peer-to-Peer Architecture
- **Dynamic roles:** Any peer can act as client or server.
- **No central authority** (in pure P2P).
- **Self-scaling:** More peers → more aggregate bandwidth.
- **Examples:** BitTorrent, Gnutella, Chord, Bitcoin, Skype.

### 2.1.3 Hybrid P2P Architecture
- **Central lookup index** but file transfer is peer-to-peer.
- **Examples:** Napster (central directory + direct transfer), KaZaA.

## 2.2 Classification of P2P Networks

### A. Based on Centralization

| Type | Central Server? | Look-up | Data Transfer | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Centralized P2P** | Yes (directory only) | Via server | Peer ↔ Peer | Napster |
| **Decentralized / Pure P2P** | No | Flooding / DHT | Peer ↔ Peer | Gnutella, Chord |
| **Hybrid P2P** | Yes (supernodes) | Via supernodes | Peer ↔ Peer | KaZaA, Skype |

### B. Based on Structure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Unstructured P2P:** No precise control over file placement. Search uses flooding or random walks. *Robust, but inefficient search.*
> - **Structured P2P:** Files placed at specific locations using **Distributed Hash Tables (DHT)**. *Efficient O(log N) lookup, but higher maintenance cost.*

## 2.3 Detailed Walkthrough — Famous P2P Systems

### 1️⃣ Napster (1999) — Centralized P2P
- **Mechanism:** Central server maintained an index of files (file name → IP address mapping).
- **Process:** Peer A searches the index on the central server → server returns IP of peer B → A downloads directly from B.
- **Weakness:** Server raid in 2001 killed it (single point of failure).

### 2️⃣ Gnutella (2000) — Pure Decentralized P2P
- **Mechanism:** No central server. Uses **flooding** (TTL-limited query propagation).
- **Process:** Query sent to all neighbors → neighbors forward to their neighbors → up to TTL.
- **Weakness:** Massive query traffic; scalability issues.

### 3️⃣ BitTorrent (2001) — Modern P2P File Distribution
- **Mechanism:** A `.torrent` file contains metadata; the **tracker** coordinates peers in a "swarm."
- **Torrents are split into pieces (chunks)**, typically **256 KB** each.
- **Tit-for-Tat Policy:** Peers prefer sending chunks to those who recently sent chunks to them (incentive mechanism).
- **Rarest-First Strategy:** Peer requests the rarest chunk among its neighbors first → balances replication.

### 4️⃣ DHT / Chord (2001) — Structured P2P
- **Mechanism:** Each peer/node assigned a random **m-bit ID** from a circular keyspace $[0, 2^m - 1]$.
- **Files (keys)** mapped to nodes using **consistent hashing**.
- **Lookup:** O(log N) hops using a finger table.
- **Example:** $m = 7 \Rightarrow$ keyspace has 128 identifiers.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — File Distribution Time

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These formulas are **examiner favorites** and appear every KTU exam cycle.

Let us denote:
- $N$ = number of peers downloading a file
- $F$ = size of the file (in bits)
- $u_s$ = server's upload rate
- $u_i$ = upload rate of peer $i$
- $d_i$ = download rate of peer $i$
- $d_{\min}$ = minimum download rate among all peers

### File Distribution Time Equations

$$
D_{C-S} \;=\; \max\!\left\{\,\frac{N \cdot F}{u_s}\,,\ \frac{F}{d_{\min}}\,\right\}
$$

$$
D_{P2P} \;=\; \max\!\left\{\,\frac{F}{u_s}\,,\ \frac{F}{d_{\min}}\,,\ \frac{N \cdot F}{u_s + \sum_{i=1}^{N} u_i}\,\right\}
$$

| Quantity | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $D_{C-S}$ | Distribution time in client-server model | seconds |
| $D_{P2P}$ | Distribution time in P2P model | seconds |
| $\dfrac{N \cdot F}{u_s}$ | Time for server to upload N copies | seconds |
| $\dfrac{F}{u_s}$ | Time for server to upload at least one copy | seconds |
| $\dfrac{F}{d_{\min}}$ | Worst-case download time for slowest peer | seconds |
| $\dfrac{N \cdot F}{u_s + \sum u_i}$ | Aggregate bandwidth bound | seconds |

### Boundary Conditions
- $D_{C-S} \geq D_{P2P}$ **always** (P2P is never slower for large $N$).
- When $N$ is small, both models perform similarly.
- When $N$ is large, $D_{P2P}$ approaches $\dfrac{F}{d_{\min}}$ (a **constant**), while $D_{C-S}$ grows **linearly** with $N$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** BitTorrent uses these principles to distribute Linux ISOs (often 4+ GB) to millions of peers in a few hours. The aggregate bandwidth scales with the swarm size, making distribution nearly *free* for the original seeder.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Client-Server Distribution Time

We derive the time to distribute a file of size $F$ to $N$ peers from a single server with upload rate $u_s$.

### Step 1 — Server Upload Bottleneck
The server must transmit **one full copy** of the file to **each** of the $N$ peers. Therefore, the total data the server must push onto the network is $N \cdot F$ bits.

The server's upload rate is $u_s$ bits/second, so the **minimum time** the server needs is:

$$
t_{\text{server, min}} = \frac{N \cdot F}{u_s}
$$

### Step 2 — Client Download Bottleneck
Each client $i$ has download rate $d_i$. The slowest client determines the worst case:

$$
t_{\text{client, min}} = \frac{F}{d_{\min}}
$$

where $d_{\min} = \min(d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_N)$.

### Step 3 — Combine Bottlenecks
Both conditions must be satisfied simultaneously. The distribution time is governed by the **slower of the two**:

$$
D_{C-S} = \max\!\left\{\frac{N \cdot F}{u_s}, \frac{F}{d_{\min}}\right\}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** The server cannot "finish" until it has uploaded N copies (uploads may finish before clients finish downloading — and vice versa). The total time is dominated by whichever task takes longer.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of P2P Distribution Time

In a P2P system, the server only needs to upload **one copy** to *some* peer, and then peers help redistribute.

### Step 1 — Server Upload Bottleneck
The server must upload at least **one copy** of the file (say to the first peer). Hence:

$$
t_{\text{server, min}} = \frac{F}{u_s}
$$

### Step 2 — Client Download Bottleneck (Unchanged)
The slowest client still dictates the floor:

$$
t_{\text{client, min}} = \frac{F}{d_{\min}}
$$

### Step 3 — Aggregate Upload Bottleneck (NEW!)
Total data to push into the network = $N \cdot F$ bits.

Total upload capacity of the system = $u_s$ (server) + $u_1 + u_2 + \cdots + u_N$ (peers, but a peer can only upload what it has already received — modeled by the aggregate ceiling).

Therefore:

$$
t_{\text{aggregate, min}} = \frac{N \cdot F}{u_s + \sum_{i=1}^{N} u_i}
$$

### Step 4 — Combine All Three Bottlenecks

$$
D_{P2P} = \max\!\left\{\frac{F}{u_s}, \frac{F}{d_{\min}}, \frac{N \cdot F}{u_s + \sum_{i=1}^{N} u_i}\right\}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why P2P Wins:** As $N$ grows, $\sum u_i$ grows with it. The aggregate bound becomes much smaller than $\dfrac{N \cdot F}{u_s}$. Eventually, $\dfrac{F}{d_{\min}}$ dominates, making $D_{P2P}$ **independent of $N$**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** Suppose $F = 10$ Gbits, $N = 10$ peers, $u_s = 100$ Mbps, $u_i = 1$ Mbps (each peer), $d_{\min} = 10$ Mbps. Compute $D_{C-S}$ and $D_{P2P}$.

### Step 1 — Normalize Units
Let us convert everything to **bits and seconds**:
- $F = 10 \times 10^9$ bits = $10^{10}$ bits
- $u_s = 100 \times 10^6$ bps = $10^8$ bps
- $u_i = 1 \times 10^6$ bps = $10^6$ bps
- $d_{\min} = 10 \times 10^6$ bps = $10^7$ bps

### Step 2 — Compute Client-Server Time

$$
\frac{N \cdot F}{u_s} = \frac{10 \times 10^{10}}{10^8} = \frac{10^{11}}{10^8} = 1000 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\frac{F}{d_{\min}} = \frac{10^{10}}{10^7} = 1000 \text{ s}
$$

$$
D_{C-S} = \max(1000, 1000) = 1000 \text{ s}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute P2P Time

$$
\frac{F}{u_s} = \frac{10^{10}}{10^8} = 100 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\frac{F}{d_{\min}} = 1000 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\frac{N \cdot F}{u_s + \sum u_i} = \frac{10^{11}}{10^8 + 10 \times 10^6} = \frac{10^{11}}{1.1 \times 10^8} \approx 909.1 \text{ s}
$$

$$
D_{P2P} = \max(100, 1000, 909.1) = 1000 \text{ s}
$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation
- $D_{C-S} = 1000$ s
- $D_{P2P} = 1000$ s
- **Why equal?** The slowest client ($d_{\min}$) is the bottleneck for *both*. P2P wins decisively only when $d_{\min}$ is large and $N$ is large.

> [!NOTE]
> **Try this:** Recalculate with $d_{\min} = 100$ Mbps and $N = 100$. You will see $D_{P2P} \approx 100$ s, while $D_{C-S} = 10000$ s — a **100×** improvement!

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Client-Server | Pure P2P | Hybrid P2P |
|---|---|---|---|
| Scalability | Poor (server bottleneck) | Excellent (self-scaling) | Good |
| Single point of failure | Yes (server) | No | Partial (supernode) |
| Search efficiency | High (indexed) | Low (flooding) or O(log N) (DHT) | Medium |
| File integrity | Centralized trust | Trustless (chunks hash-verified) | Medium |
| Complexity | Low | High (overlay maintenance) | Medium |
| Example | HTTP web server | BitTorrent, Gnutella | Napster, KaZaA |

## 4.1 Client-Server vs P2P Architecture

The following block-level functional diagram contrasts the two architectures at a high level.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph cs["CLIENT SERVER ARCHITECTURE"]
        direction LR
        c1["CLIENT 1"] -- "REQUEST" --> sv["CENTRAL SERVER"]

```

```mermaid

        pB -- "DOWNLOAD FROM" --> pD["PEER D"]
        pB -- "DOWNLOAD FROM" --> pE["PEER E"]
        pC -- "DOWNLOAD FROM" --> pD
        pC -- "DOWNLOAD FROM" --> pE

        pE -- "DOWNLOAD FROM" --> pA
    end
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph hybrid["HYBRID P2P ARCHITECTURE"]
        direction TB
        idx["CENTRAL DIRECTORY INDEX"]
        p1["PEER 1"] -- "QUERY FILE XYZ" --> idx
        idx -- "PEER 5 HAS XYZ" --> p1
        p1 -- "DIRECT TRANSFER XYZ" --> p5["PEER 5"]
        p2["PEER 2"] -- "QUERY FILE XYZ" --> idx

        p3["PEER 3"] -- "QUERY FILE XYZ" --> idx

    end
```

## 4.4 BitTorrent Swarm — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph swarm["BITTORRENT SWARM"]
        direction TB
        tr["TRACKER SERVER"]
        se["SEEDER HAS FULL FILE"]
        pX["PEER X PARTIAL FILE"]
        pY["PEER Y PARTIAL FILE"]
        pZ["PEER Z PARTIAL FILE"]
    end
    pX -- "PEER LIST" --> tr
    pY -- "PEER LIST" --> tr
    pZ -- "PEER LIST" --> tr
    se -- "CHUNK UPLOAD" --> pX
    se -- "CHUNK UPLOAD" --> pY
    pX -- "TIT FOR TAT CHUNK" --> pY
    pY -- "TIT FOR TAT CHUNK" --> pZ
    pZ -- "TIT FOR TAT CHUNK" --> pX
```

## 4.5 DHT Chord Ring — Structured P2P Lookup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ring["CHORD RING M EQUALS 7"]
        direction LR
        n0["NODE 0"] -- "FINGER" --> n8["NODE 8"]
        n0 -- "SUCCESSOR" --> n1["NODE 1"]
        n1 -- "SUCCESSOR" --> n3["NODE 3"]
        n3 -- "SUCCESSOR" --> n7["NODE 7"]
        n7 -- "SUCCESSOR" --> n12["NODE 12"]
        n12 -- "SUCCESSOR" --> n0
    end
    q["QUERY FOR KEY 10"] -- "HASH ROUTE" --> n0
    n0 -- "FINGER FORWARD" --> n8
    n8 -- "FINGER FORWARD" --> n12
    n12 -- "RESPONSE WITH VALUE" --> q
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** In Chord, a query for key $k$ is routed to the node whose ID is the *first successor* of $k$ on the ring. Finger tables reduce the routing distance from $O(N)$ to $O(\log N)$.

**Differentiate between Client-Server and Peer-to-Peer architectures with suitable examples.**

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**What is a Distributed Hash Table (DHT)? Mention its role in structured P2P networks.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

A **Distributed Hash Table (DHT)** is a decentralized key-value lookup system used in structured P2P networks. Each key is hashed and assigned to a specific node; lookups are resolved in $O(\log N)$ hops using finger tables (as in Chord). DHT provides **scalable, fault-tolerant** name-to-location mapping without a central server.

*(Award 1 mark for DHT definition, 1 mark for O(log N) property, 1 mark for example like Chord.)*

### ✍️ QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **three architectures** of Peer-to-Peer networks with diagrams. Compare **Napster, Gnutella, and BitTorrent** in terms of centralization and search mechanism.

#### Model Solution:

**Three Architectures of P2P:**

1. **Centralized P2P (e.g., Napster)**
   - A central server maintains a *directory* of files → peer mappings.
   - File transfer happens **peer-to-peer** (direct).
   - **Pros:** Fast lookups. **Cons:** Server is single point of failure.

2. **Decentralized / Pure P2P (e.g., Gnutella)**
   - **No central server**; peers form an overlay.
   - Search via **flooding**: query broadcast up to TTL.
   - **Pros:** Robust. **Cons:** Massive query traffic, inefficient.

3. **Hybrid P2P (e.g., KaZaA, modern Skype)**
   - **Supernodes** (powerful peers) act as local directories.
   - Reduces load compared to pure flooding while staying decentralized.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Napster | Gnutella | BitTorrent |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Centralization** | Centralized index | Fully decentralized | Tracker-based (hybrid) |
| **Search** | Server lookup | Flooding (TTL) | Tracker + swarm |
| **File Transfer** | Direct P2P | Direct P2P | Chunk-based P2P |
| **Scalability** | Limited | Low (flooding) | High (chunks) |

**Diagram** *(Refer to Section 4.2 for Pure P2P diagram — student should draw a labelled version).*

*(Award 2 marks for each architecture explanation, 1 mark for diagram, 2 marks for comparison table.)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A file of size $F = 4$ Gbits is to be distributed to $N = 8$ peers. The server has upload rate $u_s = 32$ Mbps. Each peer has upload rate $u_i = 4$ Mbps and download rate $d_i = 16$ Mbps. Compute and compare the **distribution time** for the **Client-Server** and **P2P** models.

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — Convert Units**
- $F = 4 \times 10^9$ bits
- $u_s = 32 \times 10^6$ bps
- $u_i = 4 \times 10^6$ bps (per peer)
- $d_{\min} = 16 \times 10^6$ bps

**Step 2 — Client-Server Model**

$$
D_{C-S} = \max\!\left\{\frac{N \cdot F}{u_s}, \frac{F}{d_{\min}}\right\}
$$

$$
\frac{N \cdot F}{u_s} = \frac{8 \times 4 \times 10^9}{32 \times 10^6} = \frac{32 \times 10^9}{32 \times 10^6} = 1000 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\frac{F}{d_{\min}} = \frac{4 \times 10^9}{16 \times 10^6} = 250 \text{ s}
$$

$$
D_{C-S} = \max(1000, 250) = 1000 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 3 — P2P Model**

$$
D_{P2P} = \max\!\left\{\frac{F}{u_s}, \frac{F}{d_{\min}}, \frac{N \cdot F}{u_s + \sum u_i}\right\}
$$

$$
\frac{F}{u_s} = \frac{4 \times 10^9}{32 \times 10^6} = 125 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\frac{F}{d_{\min}} = 250 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\sum u_i = 8 \times 4 \times 10^6 = 32 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}
$$

$$
\frac{N \cdot F}{u_s + \sum u_i} = \frac{32 \times 10^9}{32 \times 10^6 + 32 \times 10^6} = \frac{32 \times 10^9}{64 \times 10^6} = 500 \text{ s}
$$

$$
D_{P2P} = \max(125, 250, 500) = 500 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 4 — Comparison**

| Model | Distribution Time |
|---|---|
| Client-Server | **1000 s** |
| P2P | **500 s** |

**P2P is 2× faster** in this scenario. The aggregate bound dominates because the peers collectively match the server's upload capacity.

*(Award 2 marks for formula setup, 3 marks for correct calculations, 2 marks for comparison interpretation.)*

---

### ✍️ QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **working of BitTorrent**. Discuss the **Tit-for-Tat** policy and **Rarest-First** chunk selection strategy.

#### Model Solution:

**Working of BitTorrent:**

1. A user downloads a `.torrent` metadata file containing:
   - Tracker URL
   - File info hash
   - Piece size (typically **256 KB**)
2. The BitTorrent client contacts the **tracker** → receives a list of peers in the **swarm**.
3. The file is split into **pieces (chunks)**.
4. Peers download missing pieces from multiple other peers simultaneously.
5. As soon as a peer finishes downloading a piece, it can start **uploading** that piece to others.

**Tit-for-Tat Policy:**
- A peer **prefers uploading** to peers that have recently uploaded to it.
- This creates a **reciprocity-based incentive** to prevent "free-riders."
- Periodically (every 10 s), peers re-evaluate the top 4 fastest "uploading-to-me" peers and unchoke them.
- An **optimistic unchoke** slot is rotated every 30 s to discover potentially better peers.

**Rarest-First Strategy:**
- Among all pieces not yet owned, the peer requests the piece that is **rarest** in the swarm.
- This **balances replication**, ensuring no piece becomes a bottleneck.
- Once all pieces are in progress, the peer switches to **random piece selection**.

**Diagram** *(Refer to Section 4.4 — BitTorrent Swarm diagram.)*

*(Award 2 marks for working explanation, 2 marks for Tit-for-Tat, 2 marks for Rarest-First, 1 mark for diagram.)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Chord protocol** for structured P2P networks. With $m = 4$ bits, illustrate how a query for **key $K = 9$** is routed using finger tables.

#### Model Solution:

**Chord Protocol Overview:**

Chord is a **structured P2P** protocol that uses **consistent hashing** to map keys to nodes.

- **Identifier Space:** $[0, 2^m - 1]$ forms a circular ring.
- Each **node** has a random $m$-bit ID.
- Each **key** is hashed to an $m$-bit value.
- A key $K$ is stored at the **first node whose ID $\geq K$** on the ring (successor).

**Finger Table** (for node $n$):
- Entry $i$ points to the first node that succeeds $n + 2^{i-1} \pmod{2^m}$.
- Enables routing in $O(\log N)$ hops.

**Illustration with $m = 4$:** (Keyspace = 0 to 15)

Assume nodes: **0, 1, 7, 12**
Assume keys mapped → successors:
- $K=9$ → successor = node **12** (smallest node ID $\geq 9$)

**Routing $K=9$ from node 0:**
- Node 0's finger table points forward to nodes like 1, 3, 7, 12.
- Node 0 forwards to **node 7** (closest predecessor of 9 it knows).
- Node 7's finger table points to **node 12**.
- Node 12 = successor of $K=9$ → returns the value.

**Path:** $0 \rightarrow 7 \rightarrow 12$ (2 hops for 4 nodes — $O(\log N)$ confirmed).

**Properties of Chord:**

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Routing complexity | $O(\log N)$ |
| Maintenance on join/leave | $O(\log^2 N)$ |
| Fault tolerance | High (multiple successors) |

*(Award 2 marks for Chord concept, 2 marks for finger table logic, 2 marks for worked routing, 1 mark for properties.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $F/u_s$ term in P2P:** Many students omit $\dfrac{F}{u_s}$ and write only the aggregate term. You will lose 2 marks. *Always include all three terms in the max() expression.*
> 2. **Unit conversion errors:** Mixing Mbps with MB, or forgetting to convert Gbits to bits. *Show explicit unit conversion steps; partial credit depends on it.*
> 3. **Confusing unstructured with structured P2P:** Gnutella = unstructured; Chord = structured. Examiners penalize this swap.
> 4. **Drawing the wrong architecture diagram:** A client-server diagram when asked for P2P is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 5. **Skipping the slowest-peer term $F/d_{\min}$:** This term is non-negotiable in both models.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **P2P Definition:** Architecture where every node is both client and server (servent model).
- ✅ **Three P2P Architectures:** Centralized (Napster), Pure Decentralized (Gnutella), Hybrid (KaZaA, BitTorrent).
- ✅ **Two P2P Categories:** Unstructured (flooding) vs Structured (DHT).
- ✅ **Client-Server Formula:** $D_{C-S} = \max\!\left\{\dfrac{NF}{u_s},\ \dfrac{F}{d_{\min}}\right\}$
- ✅ **P2P Formula:** $D_{P2P} = \max\!\left\{\dfrac{F}{u_s},\ \dfrac{F}{d_{\min}},\ \dfrac{NF}{u_s + \sum u_i}\right\}$
- ✅ **BitTorrent Tools:** **Tit-for-Tat** (reciprocity) + **Rarest-First** (balanced replication).
- ✅ **Chord Ring:** $O(\log N)$ lookup using **finger tables** over a $2^m$-sized circular identifier space.
- ✅ **Self-scaling Property:** P2P gets *faster* as peers join; Client-Server gets *slower*.
- ✅ **For $N$ large, $D_{P2P} \to F/d_{\min}$ — a constant independent of $N$!**
- ✅ **Examples to remember:** Napster (centralized), Gnutella (flooding), KaZaA (supernodes), BitTorrent (chunks + tracker), Chord (DHT), Bitcoin (P2P blockchain).
